# Thread: Possible Gall Bladder Attack?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/possible-gall-bladder-attack.969/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-04-18T20:43:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/possible-gall-bladder-attack.969/post-16291>

I have been eating post leptin RX for abt 3 weeks, did Leptin Reset from late Jan to beginning April. I am comfortably eating a BAB around 6am, and then early dinner tween 4-6 pm. Fairly high fat from CO, fatty GF animal protein, some seafood, eggs, very little veggies, dark choc bark (I make a blend of 90-100% with CO). Been eating Paleo almost 3 yrs. Started krill and vit D, 3g and 10K iu. abt a month ago.

Cold baths to mid abdomen for sev weeks, ice packs in various spots (including abdomen) as well.

Yesterday I had abdominal pain that was so bad I couldn't stand up straight, but laying down didn't really help. I had eaten my usual amount for dinner, but I felt really FULL. Stuffed. I tasted dinner that I had made for other family members who were eating later, (a few bites of sausage, which I have eaten before) and in minutes I felt like I was going to explode. I took a cold bath, put ice packs on belly, to no avail. Felt pregnant, like I couldn't take a deep breath. Looked pregnant, belly very distended. And when I forced a deep breath, it was even worse. Had to get out of tub after ~30 min, reclining on my back was not good. In bed on my left side gave some relief. Oh and burping like a pig. Really loud, just over and over. These "spells" have happened on and off over the past few years, pre-Paleo and after Paleo.

THE BURPING: started in September. I started powerlifting in then, had never really worn a lifting belt, and I remember asking the coach if wearing it would make me burp. He laughed and said he never heard of that. So since Sept, I burp every day, all damn day long. Re-tasting my food. Gross.

I suppose I thought it was just an annoyance. Did not think to see GI dr, or think it could be my gallbladder. My mother had hers out when she was 40 (my age now) I now have an appt on May3rd to see doc, wondering what can I do in the meantime, what tests can/should I ask for, and for others with similar experiences?

TODAY: belly only a little distended, no pain, burping a lot, as per usual.

### ealachan — 2012-04-18T21:26:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/possible-gall-bladder-attack.969/post-16314>

That sounds a whole lot like what my gallbladder put me through when it started going to hell, especially the part about the deep breaths causing pain and the not being able to lay on your back or right side. I dealt with a very diseased gallbladder for almost 7 months because of some miscommunication between the surgeon I saw about it and his front-office staff...but suffice it to say, it was pretty effing miserable.

I am not a doctor, but if you get to a point where you're in the worst pain of your life, your stools turn grey / white, or you start running a fever while having similar pain, you should probably go to the ER. These are all signs of a major infection and/or a stone lodged in your common bile duct, which is SERIOUS STUFF and should not be messed with.

eta: chances are if you go to the doctor and describe what's been going on, they'll test your liver function anyway...but if they don't mention it, ask for a liver function panel. That will tell them whether or not your gallbladder is working. They may also want to do an ultrasound on it so that they can see what kind of stones and level of inflammation you've got.

### nonchalant — 2012-04-18T21:59:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/possible-gall-bladder-attack.969/post-16327>

I'm surprised you would be having gall bladder problems, after eating animal fats (paleo) for a couple years. The gall bladder would be getting a good workout with that diet, nothing would stagnate and form stones. I know eating grains will tend to make the gall bladder stop contracting as well, but again, that wouldn't be the problem.

It would be a good idea to have it checked out asap, but there's no way I would allow them to remove my gall bladder, for a simple stone problem. Perhaps you have some sort of bacteria causing the gas? I sure hope you get better, it sounds terribly painful.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-19T01:58:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/possible-gall-bladder-attack.969/post-16385>

I'm surprised too, nonchalant. A bit upset about it actually. I haven't had grains since last week of December, when I had a choc chip cookie and a slice of cheesecake. I was fine then. How would I find out about a bacteria causing the gas?

DH and I have been together 3 yrs. To his recall, I have had this happen about 4 times since we've been together. It lasts about 3-5 hrs, always in evening. First time I remember it happening I was about 30, had just returned to college, thought it was from greasy eggplant parm I had eaten. (wasn't paleo then)

I didn't have fever last night, or pale stools.

Thanks for the heads up on liver function tests, ealachan. Will do. I think I will read the leaky gut Rx, I just made some beef broth on the weekend, it can't hurt to have some now.

### nonchalant — 2012-04-23T03:37:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/possible-gall-bladder-attack.969/post-17947>

Hi Teenie, how's it going?

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-23T13:48:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/possible-gall-bladder-attack.969/post-18022>

Much better, I think. I was able to get in to see GI doc on Thursday, did abdominal U/S, told me to ditch \*all\* caffeine, take 1 oz aloe vera juice 2xday and gave me scrip for nexium (2 outta 3 ain't bad, huh?) He is fine with me eating paleo, in fact he said carbs are generally the culprit in GI disorders, and good thing I am eating low carb.

I havent had the pain since, but I still feel bloated and uncomfortable in the entire upper/lower GI area. I did not start the Nexium, but I did stop caffeine and start the aloe juice. I also started drinking bone broth, about a cup a day, some spoonfuls of fermented sauerkraut too.

I'm going to eliminate eggs this week to see if that helps with the bloating/weight gain. I have gained FIVE POUNDS (!) this week since all this started, I have been CT'ing like mad since this abdominal stuff started, hoping to stop it, instead it seems worse?!? (the bloating, not pain thank god!) Yesterday I was in 60 bath for 2.5 hrs total, in addition to ice packs. Just gonna keep on truckin'

Thanks for checking in on me, nonchalant!

### Destiny — 2012-04-24T02:32:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/possible-gall-bladder-attack.969/post-18218>

He is fine with me eating paleo, in fact he said carbs are generally the culprit in GI disorders, and good thing I am eating low carb.

>
>
>
> I'm going to eliminate eggs this week to see if that helps with the bloating/weight gain. I have gained FIVE POUNDS (!) this week since all this started, I have been CT'ing like mad since this abdominal stuff started, hoping to stop it, instead it seems worse?!? (the bloating, not pain thank god!) Yesterday I was in 60 bath for 2.5 hrs total, in addition to ice packs. Just gonna keep on truckin'
>
>
>
> Thanks for checking in on me, nonchalant!

You could be detoxing at the same time. Wow, your GI doctor sounds great!

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-24T03:07:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/possible-gall-bladder-attack.969/post-18227>

Slowly, I think the docs are catching on... I sat in on a diabetes lecture a few months ago at work (i work in cardiac rehab in a major metropolitan hospital) the cardiologist was speaking about diabetics patients and talking about the need to minimize CARBS, wow, and that cholesterol labs should not have the weight and importance that they are given.

I have a cardiologist, but the labs she ran were just straight chol panel, this guy (the speaker) runs the panel that differentiates the types of LDL and does the HS CRP too ( I know because I have some of his patients and he is very thorough with their labs.) He also prescribes vit D and CoQ10, which sounds like a no brainer, but most cardiologists do not. If/when the need arises, I will switch to this guy.

And my dad's neurologist, put him on NAC, Vit C, B Complex, and upped his fish oil, in addition to his scrip for Aricept. (he has vascular dementia)

So they are catching on.

# Thread: CT and single digit bodyfat?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-single-digit-bodyfat.970/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Ben — 2012-04-18T20:47:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-single-digit-bodyfat.970/post-16295>

Just wandering if anyone has opinions on whether CT times need to be adapted when below 10% bf?

I'm about 8% body fat, 5 weeks in to CT and have made good progress... 50 degree water no longer feels that cold on entry and the post bath warm-ups are pretty easy now.

But what I struggle with is duration and I wander if this is due to low bodyfat?

At 10-15 minutes I start to shiver aggressively and by the 20-25 minute mark I'm done. I reached this duration pretty early on but have failed to increase time.

I'm using CT predominately to help with psoriasis (autoimmune condition) and wander how important it is I push for longer durations.

I know Jack recommends achieving 45 minute baths but is this predominately for fat loss? He also mentioned adjusting times depending on goals and bf%...

Any thoughts would be appreciated

### donkjellberg — 2012-04-18T20:50:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-single-digit-bodyfat.970/post-16296>

I am interested in this too since my Bf is a few digits higher than yours.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-18T21:06:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-single-digit-bodyfat.970/post-16308>

He gave a rough maintenance suggestion of (bmi X minutes) three times a week with water at 50 being the goal. I'm interested to see what his "deep ct" finding are and if there is marked differences in results in colder vs warmer waters.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-04-18T21:07:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-single-digit-bodyfat.970/post-16309>

Intuitively, I don't think it should matter. #1 WAT does not act like insulation. #2 if you're eating a high fat meal before the CT, then there should be plenty of substrate available for the BAT to make heat.

### hellojtm — 2012-04-18T23:49:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-single-digit-bodyfat.970/post-16350>

Good post. I'm not quite that low in body fat but had a similar 25 minute comfort threshold. I added ice to decrease the water temp below 50f and spot icing and I plowed through to 45 minutes. Prior to that my times had been decreasing.... I had become a CT clockwatcher....

However, it's not clear that you need to exceed those times to adapt unless you are in a rush. I would suspect that I can now do 2-3x a week at 50f for 20 minutes to remain adapted if I want to during summer. That may even be excessive. I think the extended times are for calorie wasting which I want no part of!

best,

jesse

### PaleoMom — 2012-04-19T00:55:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-single-digit-bodyfat.970/post-16364>

I don't think low bodyfat should keep you from staying in longer, just take it slow and extend your sessions as your body can handle it. I think Dr. K is telling us to work up to longer times and then once that is comfortable and if you feel like your health and body comp are optimal then you can back off on frequency. I certainly wouldn't intend on only 20-25 minutes as a final goal if you are still having autoimmune problems.

On a separate note, if you're still eating nightshades cutting those out should aid in healing autoimmune conditions.

### shilohman — 2012-04-19T01:00:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-single-digit-bodyfat.970/post-16367>

> **Ben;16365 wrote:** Just wandering if anyone has opinions on whether CT times need to be adapted when below 10% bf?
>
>
>
> I'm about 8% body fat, 5 weeks in to CT and have made good progress... 50 degree water no longer feels that cold on entry and the post bath warm-ups are pretty easy now.
>
>
>
> But what I struggle with is duration and I wander if this is due to low bodyfat?
>
>
>
>
>
> At 10-15 minutes I start to shiver aggressively and by the 20-25 minute mark I'm done. I reached this duration pretty early on but have failed to increase time.
>
>
>
> I'm using CT predominately to help with psoriasis (autoimmune condition) and wander how important it is I push for longer durations.
>
>
>
> I know Jack recommends achieving 45 minute baths but is this predominately for fat loss? He also mentioned adjusting times depending on goals and bf%...
>
>
>
>
>
> Any thoughts would be appreciated

Curious to know, does the 8% bf, which I assume is WAT turn to BAT faster if the bmi is low?

### Onum — 2012-05-13T23:48:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-single-digit-bodyfat.970/post-23751>

Sorry to resurrect an old thread but I'm in a similar boat. I'm a 5'9" 161lb (was 157 before CT(!)) 47 y/o male at approx 12% body fat who's been full immersion (head excepted) CT'ing for 4 weeks (i.e. 28 sessions) - I've been using tap water which is 50F initially but to save water not emptying for 3 sessions and temp tends to rise to 59F in an unheated bathroom.

Like Ben I feel comfortable up to about 15mins then I experience involuntary tremors in my abdomen/trunk which become increasingly severe and spread to hips/legs/arms/chest/neck over time. I have tried to "tough it out" to up to an hour but I'm practically "fitting" in the tub due to the severity of the shivering. For the fourth week I've been stopping as soon as the tremors reach an amplitude that turns into a shake which corresponds to the 15-20min window.

This seems like a fairly low challenge in terms of time/temp and anecdotally I have tolerated long cold showers before much better when "fatter" than I am now.

Having said this, I believe I "was" reasonably cold adapted before I started the CT sessions as I can tolerate cold (air) temps well and hardly bother with heating in the house irrespective of weather.

So is water immersion just "too" challenging for low body fat individuals - at least initially?

### jbond56 — 2012-05-14T00:01:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-single-digit-bodyfat.970/post-23754>

Onum,

What skin temps are you getting from your CT sessions?

### AKMan — 2012-05-14T05:00:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-single-digit-bodyfat.970/post-23807>

> **Onum;23833 wrote:** Sorry to resurrect an old thread but I'm in a similar boat. I'm a 5'9" 161lb (was 157 before CT(!)) 47 y/o male at approx 12% body fat who's been full immersion (head excepted) CT'ing for 4 weeks (i.e. 28 sessions) - I've been using tap water which is 50F initially but to save water not emptying for 3 sessions and temp tends to rise to 59F in an unheated bathroom.
>
>
>
> Like Ben I feel comfortable up to about 15mins then I experience involuntary tremors in my abdomen/trunk which become increasingly severe and spread to hips/legs/arms/chest/neck over time. I have tried to "tough it out" to up to an hour but I'm practically "fitting" in the tub due to the severity of the shivering. For the fourth week I've been stopping as soon as the tremors reach an amplitude that turns into a shake which corresponds to the 15-20min window.
>
>
>
> This seems like a fairly low challenge in terms of time/temp and anecdotally I have tolerated long cold showers before much better when "fatter" than I am now.
>
>
>
> Having said this, I believe I "was" reasonably cold adapted before I started the CT sessions as I can tolerate cold (air) temps well and hardly bother with heating in the house irrespective of weather.
>
>
>
> So is water immersion just "too" challenging for low body fat individuals - at least initially?

When I first tried cold baths, I used my hot tub turned down to 70. Air temp was in the 40s. I would start to shiver in 10-15 minutes. Since switching to indoor (70 deg air) tub, I can tolerate 33 degree water for up to 45 minutes. Keepng head and upper chest warm make a big difference to me.

### Onum — 2012-05-14T23:23:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-single-digit-bodyfat.970/post-24006>

> **AKMan;23889 wrote:** When I first tried cold baths, I used my hot tub turned down to 70. Air temp was in the 40s. I would start to shiver in 10-15 minutes. Since switching to indoor (70 deg air) tub, I can tolerate 33 degree water for up to 45 minutes. Keepng head and upper chest warm make a big difference to me.

That's quite interesting AKMan, I have noticed that when the ambient air temp is lower I do seem to succumb quicker irrespective of how comfortable I feel before getting into the bath. As an experiment, for todays CT I wore a hat which I'm sure has been mentioned before for making a difference - and I lasted a good 5 minutes longer - who'd have thought?

I'm a bit reluctant to turn the heating on just to make my CT sessions more bearable - seems a bit counter-productive to me

Anyway, we have been "enjoying" some unseasonably cold weather in the UK recently so maybe things will be better when spring arrives proper. 70F room temp sounds unfeasibly warm to me at the moment (I set my roomstat at about 50F although it's below the minimum dot so can't tell exactly - not that it makes any difference as I don't turn the heating on anyway!)

### donkjellberg — 2012-05-14T23:32:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-single-digit-bodyfat.970/post-24008>

Temperature fluctuations have a significant impact on our perception and tolerance of CT. We can tweak variables such as ambient temperature to affect CT and I remember Dr. K indicating he has done this in the past with his n=1 experiments.

I don't have a lot of weight to lose (15-20 lbs), but I feel great since doing the Leptin Rx and notice that I feel even better after my CT sessions. # Thread: Stroke recovery advice
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stroke-recovery-advice.973/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-04-18T23:38:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stroke-recovery-advice.973/post-16345>

I seem to remember reading something about stroke recovery in the comments section of a blog, but couldn't find it now.

A dear friend, 49 years old, had a stroke in November and still has some paralysis on her left side. She has massage, occupational & speech therapy 2X/wk, but isn't improving much. She still has high bp & high cholesterol.

She seems open to CT & Paleo, but I'm not sure I explained it very well and I'm afraid I may have forgotten things. I couldn't simplify it enough for someone who is overwhelmed with life right now. I don't feel qualified to advise her.

She does love seafood & eats grass-fed beef and was shocked when I said it was good to eat the fat too, as she'd been advised to eat a lowfat diet. She eats carbs regularly--& lots of bananas.:-(

Which would be more important for her to start with if she can't do both at first--Paleo or CT?? Would normal protocol for CT be safe for her?

I did find supplement recommendations under Levee 29 info.

My sister (53 yrs)recently had a brain hemorrhage (is that the same as a stroke?), with no visible lasting effects. Any advice would help her as well.

Thanks!

# Thread: Kidney Detox Symptom?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidney-detox-symptom.974/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### tweety — 2012-04-19T01:33:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidney-detox-symptom.974/post-16376>

Would this be a detox of my kidneys?

So the other night I did spot CT on my back. I have always done spot CT on my abdomen, and have been doing cold baths for almost two months. I have noticed that if I put my ice pack directly on my stomach it will feel a little brusied for a while. When I put it on my back, I made sure to put my shirt between the ice and my skin. The next day my back was sore, like bruised. But it's the muscle, and only on my left side. It is most tender rightwhere my ribs are, but on my back side, not the front.

In addition, I decided to let the area rest and not CT there for a while. I noticed that when I now do a bath or a spot CT on my stomach it will sometimes make the area more tender. Is there something I should do to ease the detox if it is in fact my kidney?

### PaleoMom — 2012-04-19T02:30:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidney-detox-symptom.974/post-16405>

Several of us have had this in spot CT areas. I think it is just a sign of doing a tad too much.

### tweety — 2012-04-19T03:02:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidney-detox-symptom.974/post-16418>

Well thats what I thought at first. But the location was what was stopping me!

# Thread: Is menopause a must have in life?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-menopause-a-must-have-in-life.975/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-04-19T02:03:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-menopause-a-must-have-in-life.975/post-16390>

Why menopause? Is it neolithic or?

Great minds on here have info?

### Souldanzer — 2012-04-19T02:18:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-menopause-a-must-have-in-life.975/post-16398>

Interesting question.

I think it happens when a woman runs out of eggs? ... in that case, yep, a must.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-19T02:19:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-menopause-a-must-have-in-life.975/post-16399>

From what I've read about it, menopause is unique to the human race. Animals just keep on pushing out babies till they drop dead.

I consider this evolutionary evidence that human women have things more useful to do for the survival and well-being of their immediate groups than just raising the population at the maximum possible speed.

Personally, I was delighted to stop the cramps, mess, bleeding, and emotional lability. Of course, Doc would probably say that these drawbacks stem from being "neolithic" and he might be right -- still, I think a lot of women feel like menopause is a ticket to freedom of many kinds. The freedom to do what one ought to be doing, what one is truly suited for. The freedom to choose what that is.

Whatever the purported benefits, no way is anyone going to get me to take enough hormones to start cycling again ...

### chocolate — 2012-04-19T03:17:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-menopause-a-must-have-in-life.975/post-16422>

There is an article about reversing menopause. We can grow more eggs.

[http://www.news-medical.net/news/20120409/Eggs-developed-from-stem-cells-could-change-reproductive-technology.aspx](http://http://www.news-medical.net/news/20120409/Eggs-developed-from-stem-cells-could-change-reproductive-technology.aspx)

there are certain receptors on all tissues and organs. The get alarm signals and inflammation signals from the white fat alot. The way my peabrain understands it. The brown stuff needs activated. There are tons of cold rat studies. The study above is about human eggs grown from stem cells on ovaries. Its not donor eggs.

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?1009-Leptin-production-lt-10-BF](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?1009-Leptin-production-lt-10-BF)

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-19T04:08:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-menopause-a-must-have-in-life.975/post-16439>

"Generating an unlimited supply of human eggs and the prospect of reversing [menopause](http://http://www.news-medical.net/health/What-is-Menopause.aspx) was made possible by a series of breakthroughs led by Professor Jonathan Tilly of Harvard"

Great article and amazing info to think about. I love the line (above) from the article. This is made possible by some guy at Harvard, not our own biology!

### ColdBren — 2012-04-19T04:14:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-menopause-a-must-have-in-life.975/post-16443>

lol....that harvard guy is amazing.

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-19T04:28:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-menopause-a-must-have-in-life.975/post-16449>

Looking at the cycle you have the maiden, mother, crone

The maiden is the the transition from child to woman at the onset of menses

Mother is when the woman actually becomes one

Crone is when the mother no longer can provide the sustenance in the womb or breast and transitions to an elder who helps guide the young women through the stages and supports the tribe w/child care etc. she is the midwife and shaman for the tribe

As to the physicality of stopping menopause I've never minded TOTM b/c I always too the 1st day for me (when I could swing it). I learned from the onset it was something to cherish for it was special. Given my hormonal craziness. I'd like to keep from loosing the hormones. But when I make the official transition, even if Im still on BHRT I'll wiling enter the circle of crones.

### chocolate — 2012-04-19T05:54:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-menopause-a-must-have-in-life.975/post-16491>

[http://www.google.com/url?sa=t&rct=j&q=&esrc=s&source=web&cd=4&ved=0CEUQFjAD&url=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.buzzle.com%2Farticles%2Feffects-of-high-altitude-on-the-human-body.html&ei=GHyPT9-gCqbjiAKdy5HuAg&usg=AFQjCNGwQKy\_vXuOanizKSpoB5fgcsda4Q&sig2=jGJZPCdQGI5OnV6l1pAjrg](http://http://www.google.com/url?sa=t&rct=j&q=&esrc=s&source=web&cd=4&ved=0CEUQFjAD&url=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.buzzle.com%2Farticles%2Feffects-of-high-altitude-on-the-human-body.html&ei=GHyPT9-gCqbjiAKdy5HuAg&usg=AFQjCNGwQKy_vXuOanizKSpoB5fgcsda4Q&sig2=jGJZPCdQGI5OnV6l1pAjrg)

Effects of High Altitude on the Human Body

Well all of us have insulin receptor problems, usually from too much iron in the blood apparently. My kid showed elevated risk and her blood work showed it as well. This is supposed to be the cause of macular degeneration and lots of stuff. It is a symptom of high elevations. Increased mitochondria is also. The article is good to read. Just the altitude alone causes a default to ketosis. It doesn't matter what you eat up there,( to a point.)

Macular degeneration and insulin resistance at low elevations....probably the perfect calibration for high altitude.

### Glamorama — 2012-04-19T06:29:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-menopause-a-must-have-in-life.975/post-16503>

Menopause is natural, but according to Nora G, it shouldn't be as uncomfortable as it is for most woman. She thinks a SAD-diet makes it many times worse than it would have been if we all were born and raised on Paleo cooking. She might have a point, I know I stopped having PMS and severe TOM-symthomps when I started eating Primal.

### Croak — 2012-04-19T06:35:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-menopause-a-must-have-in-life.975/post-16506>

[](http://http://www.washingtonpost.com/national/health-science/evolution-has-given-humans-a-huge-advantage-over-most-other-animals-middle-age/2012/03/12/gIQAtVnccS_print.html)

> [](http://http://www.washingtonpost.com/national/health-science/evolution-has-given-humans-a-huge-advantage-over-most-other-animals-middle-age/2012/03/12/gIQAtVnccS_print.html)
>
> [
>
> Thus, humans can be seen as members of an elite club of species in which adulthood has become so long and complicated that it can no longer all be given over to breeding. Just like farsightedness and inelastic skin, menopause now appears to be a coordinated, controlled process. It liberates women and their partners from the unremitting demands of producing children and gives them time to do what middle-aged people do best: live long and pamper.
>
> ](http://http://www.washingtonpost.com/national/health-science/evolution-has-given-humans-a-huge-advantage-over-most-other-animals-middle-age/2012/03/12/gIQAtVnccS_print.html)

http://www.washingtonpost.com/national/health-science/evolution-has-given-humans-a-huge-advantage-over-most-other-animals-middle-age/2012/03/12/gIQAtVnccS\_print.html

### LinD — 2012-04-19T06:58:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-menopause-a-must-have-in-life.975/post-16512>

While inconvenient, I would rather have my monthly cycle than not. At age 50, I am still regular and wonder hire menopause will affect me... My mother died at a young age, and my sister had a hysterectomy at age 40, so no history to pull from.

Sprint Samsung Epic

### Owl — 2012-04-19T08:37:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-menopause-a-must-have-in-life.975/post-16521>

So according to Croak's article, pilot and killer whales also go through menopause. If we're descended from the sea, then it looks like it's entirely plausible that menopause is natural and the best thing for our species. Our young take too long to grow up and too many resources for us to be having children in old age.

> Well all of us have insulin receptor problems, usually from too much iron in the blood apparently. My kid showed elevated risk and her blood work showed it as well. This is supposed to be the cause of macular degeneration and lots of stuff. It is a symptom of high elevations. Increased mitochondria is also. The article is good to read. ### chocolate — 2012-04-19T18:05:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-menopause-a-must-have-in-life.975/post-16602>

Its awfully hard to build muscle post meno. I am trying to get myself as strong as I can and the keto and cold do it best. Plus, I'm not so thrilled with waning eyesight. I guess the definition of pamper is relevant. I hate being weak and the off being balance that comes from weakness is frightening. I used to work at the airport and the people that pamper themselves are the weakest. You will see an 85 year light bit of fluff walk hard and fast for a distant gate with a wheelchair aide chasing her and the woman crabbing the whole way for them to leave them alone. If its an strong spirited old man (not the same as pushy and demanding) , you have flirt and get edgy to get him to let you "help". Then you'll have very large sixty something couples wanting someone to push the ass they don't even want to haul. I don't want to be those pampered people.

But on the flip side, the post menopausal skilled and classy are wonderful. I home schooled in LA and had my kids in several parks and rec daytime adult classes. The adults that take those classes in our area are retirees and wonderful. They share and give and enjoy, We were very blessed. The ice rink in the daytime has the same, and you get tons of free lessons from people that actually know what they are talking about. Some of my best mentoring came from college professors that had my elementary aged kids in their classes. They were peeved at first but started coaching me a little when I would hit a wall. They all hit bumps with their kids and would share how to get over them or how they might have done things differently, rethinking. I appreciated it, because I need constructive criticism.

Glad there wasn't a lipitor ad with the article.

### Croak — 2012-04-19T18:35:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-menopause-a-must-have-in-life.975/post-16613>

I think the "live long and pamper" was referring to pampering the progeny, not yourself.

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-19T20:42:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-menopause-a-must-have-in-life.975/post-16683>

I don't think menopause = running out of eggs. We're "supposed" to have only a few dozen cycles in our lives, not hundreds. Most of the eggs will go unused in that situation, no?

I think that menopause has got to be far worse now than it's supposed to be. I know for a fact that cycles are not supposed to be painful, or preceded by hormonal uproar. They're also not supposed to happen for most of our lives. Biologically, they're supposed to start around 15, be followed soon thereafter by a pregnancy, then disappear for 3 years or so till the next one or two cycles are followed by another pregnancy; rinse and repeat for a few decades.

I don't know when they're "supposed" to end, biologically, though. I'm still looking for data from traditional peoples that shows the typical age of menopause, and duration of perimenopause (which I also suspect is artificially long now).

Menopause is NOT supposed to be accompanied by hot flashes & hormonal uproar, or followed by a decline in hearing, eyesight, bone composition, etc., etc.

I read an interesting article in the Price-Pottenger Nutrition Foundation journal that gave a nice explanation of why modern "age-related" decline takes far longer and is far worse than it's supposed to be. Yes, hormones decline with age, but today they decline far too early and far too much.

I do think menopause is a gift (just as my regular intervals of 3 years without cycles for the last 15 years are a gift), and that the post-menopausal woman should be a gift to the community, not relegated to "irrelevant" status the way she has become post-60's ("Don't trust anyone over 30").

### kathylu — 2012-04-20T15:58:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-menopause-a-must-have-in-life.975/post-17003>

> **MamaGrok;16753 wrote:**
>
> I don't know when they're "supposed" to end, biologically, though. I'm still looking for data from traditional peoples that shows the typical age of menopause, and duration of perimenopause (which I also suspect is artificially long now).
>
>
>
> Menopause is NOT supposed to be accompanied by hot flashes & hormonal uproar, or followed by a decline in hearing, eyesight, bone composition, etc., etc.

Just another N=1, but my mother-in-law didn't stop regular periods until she was 66! She had 7 children, which I'm sure contributed to the late menopause. She has amazing skin and energy and looks far younger than her 80 years.

### MJ* — 2012-04-20T21:41:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/long-island-swim-meet-up.977/post-17172>

> **Jim;17086 wrote:** So which ice packs are you using? BTW I am originally from Syosset but now live in Virginia so unfortunately I can't make it. My tap water is now 60F.

I use a few different ones, but the brand Coldpac by Chattanooga is my newest...what I love about it is it's larger size...I got the 11x14 and it covers my whole stomach, and it's pliable so I can squeeze it into wherever I have freezer space...they also have a larger size that was more money that I might invest in eventually. I also picked up a face mask that you freeze (found in the beauty isle at King Kullen) I love that too, it's soothing and I'd swear the skin above my eyes is looking tighter! Then I use these smaller ones called thermacool, and what I love about these is they stay icy longer...I try to put these where the brown fat is...Why is everyone moving away? I love LI...I grew up in Hicksville, our towns bordered I think...

### Dali Dula — 2012-04-21T00:04:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/long-island-swim-meet-up.977/post-17219>

I know everybody should be moving at their own rate, but most of you should be ready to move on to full immersion. It's quicker and more effective. No ice blocks and gear. Just walk into that 55 deg water . You will love it. I find the shower harder than immersion for some reason even when the immersion temp is colder. I do ten minutes immersion every am in a pond that is 50.

### Dali Dula — 2012-04-21T00:18:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/long-island-swim-meet-up.977/post-17230>

NIce photo Dr. Kruse. Still taking baby steps over here. I have done 55 cold baths so I don't think 52 will be that different, but it will be full immersion so we'll see and I have done no CT at all this week. IN my bath, I have my arms out and shoulders out of the water, gotta hold my book to read. I stay in 30 minutes andy haven't been able to extend that more than that.

I think the hcg is making me colder this week. So Sunday will be a huge challenge and it will be down to 30s at night and maybe 40s when we're there.

Maybe I will try to meet you for BAB anyway, but I won't be eating anything. I'll make sure to have my protein before I leave. I'll see how it goes that morning.

Seriously! We live in the perfect place where we can watch the sun go down behind the mountains and sit around firelight after dark - all after a day of cold dips and whatnot.

I have a sadly deficient knowledge of the secluded mountain areas/streams. I really REALLY want to find a place. I'm very motivated to take my son camping and enjoy real dark/real quiet/real fire. And of course a cold mountain stream would rock.

Wonder if the REI folks might know, or if there are camping clubs we might ask?

### Souldanzer — 2012-04-29T01:52:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/long-island-swim-meet-up.977/post-19686>

There are spots all around you, actually. Heck, the Boulder Creek is a populated summer time dip... nobody would even notice. And still cold b/c of the snow melt. Eben J Fine park is bikini central in the summer.

There's another place farther up on the Boulder Canyon trail where there's a tiny little "beach". Always taken in the summer. Just got to be careful with the speed of the water current at times of the year with the creeks. If you go further up there's a place where people have put rocks and stuff to make a little "cold tub" that diverts the current.

Hiker over here  I wish my head wasn't still hurting like hell ... I'd be out there in no time.

Oh, and if you go up the first part of Bear Canyon Trail, past NCAR, there's another little "tub". My dog likes to go there for a dip when we pass by.

None of these places are particularly private unless you want to add a day hike. But they're not necessarily exposed, either. And who cares anyways... Keep Boulder Weird.

I desperately want that quiet with nature thing - perhaps all this CT and primal living is making me crave the nature aspect.

### Jim — 2012-04-29T15:39:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/long-island-swim-meet-up.977/post-19758>

OK I did it...here's the proof.

Sunrise

Beaverdam Reservoir

Air temp 41 deg F

Water temp 55 deg F

First let's make sure CTing is legal....

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

I see nothing in the rules that say anything about CT so it must be fine.

Now let's survey the scene.

I must say that it is beautiful at dawn. This is the boat ramp area for the crew teams.

OK remember I am taking these by myself....notice how I chopped off the top of my head in this one. I guess the camera somehow knew this was a bad hair day!

As you can see I have lost a good bit of my visceral fat but still have a lot of pesky subQ fat to go.

So Ok quit stalling...time to go in...

So I stayed in for about 15 minutes or so. It was very easy given the water temp and the lack of wind. I hope my LI brethren fared well today and weren't eaten by a horseshoe crab or something.

### TerrierMom — 2012-04-29T17:22:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/long-island-swim-meet-up.977/post-19777>

> **Cavemam;19764 wrote:** heh, I left out an important part of my "want". I don't care to be around other people! I want to sit quietly in my icy stream and read or relax. Same with camping, I never understood camping at campgrounds. Your around other people and their lights and noises etc. Seems to ruin it for me. I desperately want that quiet with nature thing - perhaps all this CT and primal living is making me crave the nature aspect.
>
>
>
> I just need a few gajillion bucks so I can buy 45 acres in the mountains by a nice snow-run-off stream. Anybody want to timeshare that?

I'm with you there..l have seen some amazing campsites but they'd only rock if nobody else was there!!

Don't joke about the timeshare it's a great idea to go in on something like that!!

I'm not a hotelier but I've already imagined building a JK style retreat... In the mountains somewhere, lots of natural streams and swimming holes for earthing style CT.... (with sunrise/sunset views of course)....Awesome JK Paleo gourmet food.... Rooms that black out for deep sleep.. Yellow lights in all the fixtures... And blue blocking glasses for those who can't stay away from the Optimal Living forum after dark ..... Deep tubs for those who CT in a tub....oh and extra ice makers!! I would be all over a retreat like that!!!

### Claudia — 2012-04-29T17:49:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/long-island-swim-meet-up.977/post-19803>

Hey, everyone. I did it. I have no idea what happened to everyone else. I had my breakfast here at home, went to Sweet Mama's to meet everyone. I did say I would be later since I wasn't eating. No one there. So, I drove to Sunken Meadow beach by myself and took the plunge, despite the many signs that said Swimming Prohibited. I took photos with my phone but couldn't get online, no reception.

No idea the water temp, but guessing low 50s. Air temp 48-50. I stayed in for 15 minutes, up to my shoulders but with my arms out, from 9:45-10am. I am home now still shivering. I am so excited that I did it !!!! I will post pics in a bit.

### Claudia — 2012-04-29T17:50:35+0300

### Claudia — 2012-04-29T18:12:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/long-island-swim-meet-up.977/post-19820>

SOrry your wife is sick, PaleoDentist. My husband pulled something in his side lifting something heavy yesterday and was moaning and groaning all day and night. I told him to ice right away and he listened but then, he had spasms and blamed it on the ice. ANyway, he said he couldn't watch the kids this morning and I hesitated and thought maybe I shouldn't go. And then, I said well, do your best, and I went away . I am proud of this, too. I never put me first. They all managed just fine. I made them breakfast before I left.

### PaleoDentist — 2012-04-29T18:15:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/long-island-swim-meet-up.977/post-19822>

Hi, my wife was throwing up all night and morning. I could not leave her. Sorry I let you all down. Hopefully e will do it again soon.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-29T18:15:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/long-island-swim-meet-up.977/post-19824>

WHAT A BUMMER about that Claudia. Well, you Jim and I did the deed! It was exciting, right?

All warmed up now, here are stats:

air temp 63, water temp 51. stayed in for 25 min, up to neck, was in a very sheltered area called the cove, right by Shark River Inlet. I wanted to be safe since I was going alone, I also checked tides, it was low tide, so cove + LT = I felt safe. I was so excited on the drive down. I marched right down to shoreline, dumped sweatshirt and ran whooping like a lunatic in a tank top and booty shorts right into the water. THe guys fishing on the jetty were laughing. I swam, treaded water, bodysurfed, and sometimes just stood still. It was wonderful. There was a nook by the jetty where I could sit with the water up to my chin, but that was boring, I wanted to move around and play. AWESOME. I will definitely do this again!

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/long-island-swim-meet-up.977/post-19909>

ChimpChick is a LOOSER!!

I was showered packed and all ready to go. I was just waiting to hear back from the girls if we were gonna reschedule or not. MJ wrote me back a 'let's go for it' but it didn't get to my inbox until 10 am with a lot of refreshing the page in between So I put my head back on the pillow and woke up in looserville!!

I'M SORRY GUYS!!

Anyway I am determined to do it later at my local beach.. MJ if you are up for another dunk I can come to your beach.. I'll see if hubbs can get some pic's too.

If you went to the creek today, let us know how it went.

### Dali Dula — 2012-04-30T04:40:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/long-island-swim-meet-up.977/post-19988>

I knew you could do it. It is better standing up and being able to move. Body surfing in the cold is a blast. I was with you all in solidarity in the 48 deg pond this morning.

So I've been doing this pond thing almost everyday for the past 30 days. Tonight i looked at my glucometer which stores the BG averages.30 day average is 119. 14 day average is 113. 7 day average is 109. 1day average(today) is 104. I am off all meds at this time.

Three years ago I was diagnosed diabetic with a A1c of 13. WTF! Everybody get in the water!

Hey, Tom, I missed this because I haven't been on. I also went in the sound on Mother's Day. I wanted to go to the beach so we went to Sunken Meadow. I didn't know what temp it was but I went all the way in this time past my shoulders and felt completely fine, stayed in for 30 minutes. My H was worried about hypothermia. I didn't have the strength to argue with him. My kids went in, too, and were fine. I imagine it was probably the same water temp as yours, around 58.

### PaleoDentist — 2012-05-27T23:15:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/long-island-swim-meet-up.977/post-27920>

Well, we finally did it! So nice to meet both of you! It was a blast, and not as hard as I thought it would be. (although I was the only one who shivered) The water was actually really nice.

Great talking to both of you and getting your real world perspectives.

MJ: very impressive for someone who never did a bath plunge!

Claudia: I am inspired how you are raising your whole family Paleo. There is hope for my son!

Hopefully we will make it at least a monthly meet up. It would be great to have others join us as well. we can chat as we ocean plunge.

My goal is to do the Long Island Polar Bear Plunge in Feb!

I'm looking forward to the pics!

Alex

### MJ* — 2012-05-28T02:28:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/long-island-swim-meet-up.977/post-27942>

Well, we finally did it! So nice to meet both of you! It was a blast, and not as hard as I thought it would be. (although I was the only one who shivered) The water was actually really nice.

It was awesome!

Great talking to both of you and getting your real world perspectives.

We all had a lot to talk about!

MJ: very impressive for someone who never did a bath plunge!

I'm telling ya, ice packs rule, get your ice packs on so you're not such a shivering wimp next time

Claudia: I am inspired how you are raising your whole family Paleo. There is hope for my son!

I agree it was really inspirational to see how you are bucking the trend!

And Alex it is obvious you really care about your patients, and their overall health...you're a very good guy!

Hopefully we will make it at least a monthly meet up. It would be great to have others join us as well. we can chat as we ocean plunge.

Yes we will definitely do this! Kristen we can't wait to meet you and anyone else that wants to ever make the trip!!!

My goal is to do the Long Island Polar Bear Plunge in Feb!

A great goal! \[

I'm looking forward to the pics!

Haha they were far away and the towel shots were not great, but the ones I took of you and Claudia look great! I will make sure she's good with them and then post, unless you want to see them first too...

# Thread: too much iron:  give blood or sell it if you can find a payer
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-iron-give-blood-or-sell-it-if-you-can-find-a-payer.979/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-04-19T04:10:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-iron-give-blood-or-sell-it-if-you-can-find-a-payer.979/post-16440>

Iron Overload Linked To Macular Degeneration - Donating Blood Could Save Your Sight

[http://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/193017.php](http://http://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/193017.php)

The most common - and under-diagnosed - genetic disease in humans just may be a cause of the worst form of macular degeneration, Medical College of Georgia researchers report.

They are pursuing a link between hemochromatosis, which results in iron overload, and the wet form of macular degeneration, the leading cause of blindness in people 60 and older. They suspect that too much iron, known to wreak cumulative havoc on the body's organs, hastens normal aging of the eyes.

### chocolate — 2012-04-19T04:11:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-iron-give-blood-or-sell-it-if-you-can-find-a-payer.979/post-16441>

I think I need magnets on my liver.....

Iron and Diabetes Revisited [http://care.diabetesjournals.org/content/34/7/1676](http://http://care.diabetesjournals.org/content/34/7/1676)

iron gunks up the receptors and causes signaling problems. That's why a few weeks on vege diet makes you feel better. These naturo paths are looking righter and righter, 30 percent protein, most of the rest fat.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-19T04:22:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-iron-give-blood-or-sell-it-if-you-can-find-a-payer.979/post-16446>

Another reason to keep drinking coffee?

### chocolate — 2012-04-19T23:39:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-iron-give-blood-or-sell-it-if-you-can-find-a-payer.979/post-16748>

> **KiwiLauren;16516 wrote: Another reason to keep drinking coffee?**

**
You nailed it. It even increases SHGB. [http://www.exercise.com/article/drink-your-joe-to-fight-diabetes](http://http://www.exercise.com/article/drink-your-joe-to-fight-diabetes)

The Research and Results

Researchers from UCLA took a subset of 359 women who were recently diagnosed with type two diabetes and 359 seemingly healthy women from the larger Women's Health Study, a study of cardiovascular disease prevention tracking more than 40,000 women. They based their research of these women on several factors:

First, researchers have long thought there was a link between the sex hormones testosterone and estrogen and the development of type two diabetes

Second, a particular protein, sex hormone–binding globulin (SHBG) is known to affect the levels of sex hormones in the bodies, acting as a regulating hormone

Researchers compared the SHBG levels of coffee drinkers and non-coffee drinkers within their subset of participants and compared those levels to the development of diabetes. Coffee drinkers had significantly higher levels of SHBG in their systems, and women who drank at least four cups of caffeinated coffee a day were 56-percent less likely to develop diabetes than their non-drinking peers.**

Unfortunately, women who stuck to decaf didn't see the same beneficial results. Apparently increased levels of SHBG has a link to the caffeine in coffee that is yet to be explained.

### chocolate — 2012-04-19T23:45:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-iron-give-blood-or-sell-it-if-you-can-find-a-payer.979/post-16751>

[http://www.livestrong.com/article/427404-caffeines-effect-on-iron-absorption/](http://http://www.livestrong.com/article/427404-caffeines-effect-on-iron-absorption/)

Polyphenols and Caffeine

Polyphenols, natural compounds found in herbs and plants, majorly inhibit iron absorption in the body. Tea leaves, coffee leaves and cocoa beans are significant polyphenol-containing products that also contain caffeine. Types of polyphenols include phenolic acid, chlorogenic acid and tannin -- all found to some degree in tea, coffee and cocoa.Other polyphenol foods include raspberries, blackberries, blueberries and apples, walnuts and spices. **The Iron Disorders Institute notes that some coffee varieties can inhibit iron absorption by as much as 60 percent.**

### venus — 2012-06-28T00:27:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-iron-give-blood-or-sell-it-if-you-can-find-a-payer.979/post-34062>

Want to give yourself...literally?

Hey, you! Yes, you gorgeous being. Are you:

Over 16?

Good, you passed. More serious questions here regarding all other aspects of your health.

But, do you want to save lives? Yes, actually save (or at least) prolong someone elseâ€™s life?

Iâ€™m talking about donating blood (but thereâ€™s also other options), and I think itâ€™s time we did something about it. Hereâ€™s some hard, statistical facts:

Only 1 in 30 Australians donate.

The blood you donate is < 10% of the total volume of blood in your body

The whole session lasts an hour, with the actual blood donating taking 10 minutes. Most of it is interview bits and resting afterwards.

27,000 donations are needed every week in Australia.

For one donation, youâ€™re not saving one, but THREE lives. How amazing is that??

Currently, blood groups O and A are needed. Iâ€™m O+, so Iâ€™ll definitely be donating.

But not everyone will go and do something about it once reading this. These are common excuses Iâ€™ve heard of and have used:

Iâ€™m dead scared of needles and pain

Yes, it can be nerve-racking the first time you donate. I did it with my school, so most of us were virgin blood donors (sounds cult-ish). I believe that if you really want to do good, you will find ways to do so. Push that boundary, face your fears. Living in fear is wasting your amazing life. Take the first step, and be brave!

I donâ€™t have the time, Iâ€™ve got other plans

Fair enough. But if you [click here](http://http://www.irondeficiencyguide.com/), youâ€™ll find that it only takes a moment to fill in your details and find your closest donating centre. Less than 5 minutes, promise.

I want to but no one wants to do it with me

Are you sure about that? Ask your family if theyâ€™re keen! Have anyone in your sports team, or club? Ask someone on your FB? Network, network, network! Grab your sibling, or just get someone to sit in with you and watch what happens. They might change their mind. I would probably go with you if you lived in or around Melbourne, though it might be a tad awkward since we both know and not-quite-know one another. Tumblr does that.

Itâ€™s not safe

Are you kidding me? The Australian Red Cross has one of the safest blood supplies in the whole world. New, sterile needles are used each time. They test you for more than just your blood type- â€œHIV/AIDs, hep B, hep C, Human T-cell Lymphotropic Virus (HTLV) and syphilisâ€. Pretty sure itâ€™s safe.

I need to book an appointment

Mostly true! You can book up to three dayâ€™s in advance. Some centres allow for you to just come in and do it, but youâ€™d have to check it out.

The last time I donated was in year 11. I wanted to in year 12, but whenever the blood bank came to school, it happened to be around SAC or exam time. Iâ€™m making a public commitment to donate every 3 months, because I can spare an hour of my day. Could you do the same? Will you step up to the challenge?

# Thread: What percentage of adults have BAT?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-percentage-of-adults-have-bat.982/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### donkjellberg — 2012-04-19T05:50:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-percentage-of-adults-have-bat.982/post-16489>

This has been bothering me for a while. I do not know if I have any BAT nor do I know what the percentage of the adult population BAT is.

I am wondering this because when I do CT, there is no way to tell if I am activating BAT or not.

I believe BAT can be recruited but do you have to have some already in order to recruit? I usually begin to shiver some time in the course of cold exposure and wonder if this is due to inability of BAT or just my body compensating for heat loss.

### nonchalant — 2012-04-19T06:02:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-percentage-of-adults-have-bat.982/post-16494>

Donkjellberg, how about taking your temperature? For the first time, I checked my oral temps several times around my CT bath yesterday. After getting in the water, my temp started to raise, and raise even higher after 20 minutes. I did shiver a little bit near the end of my 45 min bath. After getting out, my temp dropped down quite a bit, and I started to shiver. So I think my BAT was keeping me warm during the bath, but was insufficient for afterwards. Or the increase in my skin temp told the BAT to shut down.

### chocolate — 2012-04-19T06:02:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-percentage-of-adults-have-bat.982/post-16495>

I read an article and the larger you are the less you have. But you can absolutely make it. I got stiff as a robot and turned a corner, once overnight. My head was getting so hot, not like a fever, like a radiator for a few days before and I dreaded eating. Now I can give or take eating at night and sleep fine.

### donkjellberg — 2012-04-19T06:35:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-percentage-of-adults-have-bat.982/post-16507>

@ Nonchalant: I have a tympanic membrane thermometer and will use that and to see how it works. It will be hard to get a reading when I am shivering post CT, LoL.

@ chocolate: My BMI is 23 but I was larger two years ago before I lost 130 pounds. I don't know how that affects BAT loss or accumulation. More research needs to be done in this area. If cold for weight loss takes off publicly, more will be.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-04-19T15:02:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-percentage-of-adults-have-bat.982/post-16548>

Based on everything i've read, I think most if not all adults have \*some\*. But maybe if you have a very small amt, you need to take this extra slow.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-19T15:05:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-percentage-of-adults-have-bat.982/post-16550>

>

better question is this......as you are warm adapt how much BAT do you lose? And does that make it harder to convert WAT to BAT? tune in Saturday to find out.

### Clabbergirl — 2012-04-19T16:32:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-percentage-of-adults-have-bat.982/post-16579>

> **donkjellberg;16559 wrote:** This has been bothering me for a while. I do not know if I have any BAT nor do I know what the percentage of the adult population BAT is.

>

>
> I believe BAT can be recruited but do you have to have some already in order to recruit? I usually begin to shiver some time in the course of cold exposure and wonder if this is due to inability of BAT or just my body compensating for heat loss.

Thank you for asking this. I've wondered also because I've been overweight most of my life, and obese during the last 10. I can't imagine I have much BAT at all, yet I don't shiver. Body temp increases indicate BAT functioning to keep the body warm?

### donkjellberg — 2012-04-19T18:41:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-percentage-of-adults-have-bat.982/post-16614>

> **Jack Kruse;16620 wrote:** better question is this......as you are warm adapt how much BAT do you lose? And does that make it harder to convert WAT to BAT? tune in Saturday to find out.

Stop teasing!

This question is also stirring my mind. Two years ago my BMI topped out at a whopping 40.3! I always found myself sweating and know my body was adapting to its environment. If my body decreased BAT as a result, that I do not know. If it did, then perhaps it can again with new environmental influences.

I have not seen any research on the fluctuations associated with BAT during a lifetime. Since I returned to graduate school, I find myself routinely thinking of interesting questions like this. Observations can be quite easy with the right tools . . .

### AKMan — 2012-04-19T18:55:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-percentage-of-adults-have-bat.982/post-16623>

From all I've read, the way to recruit new BAT is to spend as much time as possible, minimally clothed, below the thermoneutral zone, which is about 72 degrees, and the point where shivering kicks in, 50 degrees or so.

### nonchalant — 2012-04-19T18:56:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-percentage-of-adults-have-bat.982/post-16624>

Well, I think mosts scientists assumed adults had zero BAT, until it started showing up in CAT scans. So probably no research on BAT fluctuations, at least none that is completed. I just love this cutting edge stuff!

### AKMan — 2012-04-19T19:31:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-percentage-of-adults-have-bat.982/post-16640>

> **Nonchalant;16694 wrote:** Well, I think mosts scientists assumed adults had zero BAT, until it started showing up in CAT scans. So probably no research on BAT fluctuations, at least none that is completed. I just love this cutting edge stuff!

From [http://jcem.endojournals.org/content/96/8/2450.short](http://http://jcem.endojournals.org/content/96/8/2450.short) :

"Conclusions: BAT is present in supraclavicular fat, regardless of PET status. BAT is highly prevalent in adult humans, and its abundance determines PET status."

Go to Google Scholar, type in Brown Adipose Tissue in Humans, then click the date button and only pick studies from 2011 and newer--you will be amazed!

[http://scholar.google.com/scholar?hl=en&q=Brown+adipose+in+humans&btnG=Search&as\_sdt=1%2C2&as\_ylo=2011&as\_vis=0](http://http://scholar.google.com/scholar?hl=en&q=Brown+adipose+in+humans&btnG=Search&as_sdt=1%2C2&as_ylo=2011&as_vis=0)

Thank you AKMan. I just started reading some of chocolate's posted articles on BAT and my attitude is changing! I will have to look for the full article from your first listing above. I opened up a tab with the second. That will probably keep me busy going back and forth between my schoolwork assignments and personal interests.

Another thing I have noticed, some of the PhDs I have talked to are not too familiar with epigenetics. That I need to study more in general too.

I am trying to figure out how to get a monthly MRI so I can try and see my bat and measure if it increases. Perhaps I'll look for a government grant!

### donkjellberg — 2012-04-19T23:09:39+0300

# Thread: Japanese Cold Training and the Aikido Connection
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/japanese-cold-training-and-the-aikido-connection.983/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Mei — 2012-04-19T08:35:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/japanese-cold-training-and-the-aikido-connection.983/post-16520>

I practice Ki Aikido, a non-martial art - developed in the 20th century in Japan from the more brutal Aikikai - that uses mastery and connection through ki (chi, qi, energy, the Force, whatever you want to call it) instead of physical strength to meet and overcome, or redirect, your opponent (or any energy attacking or obstructing you).

My father (also my sensei) has been listening to me yammer on about cold thermogenesis for the past month, and something finally clicked. He pulled out an old book to show me traditional Aikido cold water training, which the Ki society in Japan does every January, where everybody, including kids, goes out into freezing temperatures and has a dip in the ocean. Apparently, if you are supervised by ki-trained people, it ain't so bad. Sounds kind of familiar.

Tonight he said he found something else that said Tohei Sensei, the first teacher of our particular lineage, was a sickly boy until he began taking cold baths. He regained his health and then exceeded it, and, apparently, stumbled upon the Ancient Pathway. The dude was righteous in his day. [http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J04JI48GzPM&feature=related\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;J04JI48GzPM\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J04JI48GzPM&feature=related\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;J04JI48GzPM)

### youthfuliz — 2012-04-19T15:16:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/japanese-cold-training-and-the-aikido-connection.983/post-16556>

I think "expecting" the cold before doing it helps our body to prepare for it. Just visualizing being under the cold shower gets my heart beats faster and body temperature up.  And if I prepare my cold bath 10 minutes before hand - fill the tub, set the water to the right temperature, feel it with my hand - my body is much more eager to walk into it and the cold is less shocking.

### Guest — 2012-04-19T16:03:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/japanese-cold-training-and-the-aikido-connection.983/post-16569>

Mei--this is so fascinating and I really appreciate you bringing this forward.

Would love to know how to tap this "oneness of mind" because I am having a hard time with CT. Maybe this would be a great topic for many here.

### Mei — 2012-04-19T17:10:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/japanese-cold-training-and-the-aikido-connection.983/post-16592>

@Jack - done and done, glad you dug it! I'm pretty excited to have found this, it sort of slid the final piece of CT into my understanding. I still can't rattle off the science but if my sensei's sensei's sensei did it, then it must be right, haha!

@youthfuliz - in practice we call that extending your mind...putting your intentions a little ahead of your actions helps you see them to realization with grace and poise. You're creating an outline of your very near future and then sliding effortlessly into the space you've already made for yourself. It's no big set up or meditation or ceremony, it's just a way of being. Try it in other parts of your life (if you haven't already), mental, physical, emotional, relational...

@Suhenna - yeah, I'm pretty excited as everything in my life begins to collide...with what I believe is the Ancient Pathway. Not that I'm anywhere near there, but I can glimpse the connections with my intellectual mind and that is fascinating enough for now!! Oneness of mind is simply having your mind and body coordinated. That's harder than it sounds, because most of us are so off balance...I find each practice informs the other; i.e. as I get more cold adapted, I my ki gets stronger, and likewise as I get better at Aikido, the cold becomes more enjoyable. Likewise, I experienced huge leaps in my emotional health and clarity of mind when I did the Leptin Rx, which made Aikido far less daunting and somehow much easier to grasp. Unfortunately, I've been practicing Ki Aikido for 7 or 8 years, since I was about 14, and I still don't "have" it, it comes and goes and I have to work hard sometimes to get it happening. Try this. Right before CT, spend a moment in your body. Imagine light is radiating from your core, through your skin, and infinitely into the distance, like the sun. Then, stop thinking, and get in the tub.

OR! See if you can find some natural water to CT in. I started jumping in every river, lake, and ocean I can find (I live in BC, there are plenty around, and they are plenty cold still!), and it totally turned my CT around. What I call "real" water brings me to life, makes me joyful to be a part of it, while laying in a small tub wedged against the walls with a bag of ice is good too, but lacks a certain fun factor . I naturally extend towards the ocean, river, or lake, because my body actually wants to be a part of that flow.

I think this is why Yoga and meditation techniques have helped people do extreme things - I believe the Ancient Pathway can be accessed by various doors, many of which have been found, but so far only CT has the only door that accesses the pathway AND makes you look like a babe!

### Mei — 2012-04-19T17:35:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/japanese-cold-training-and-the-aikido-connection.983/post-16597>

Also, Suhenna: try to relax. Energy (and heat) can't flow if you're tensing up and resisting. If you notice a part of your body is full of tension (think: tight muscles, or if you're huddled or scrunched up), try to let it go. Breathe into it. We believe we need to fight against everything, we believe it so deep down that even our cells will fight something our mind considers "bad". Even if you can only relax for a second, that's enough. Next time you try, you might do a second and a half. You just slowly train your mind to find the feeling. It's the same when you're shivering, if you can concentrate for one moment to stop shivering. You'll start again, but many people have found they can do this. It's all good practice, but there's no quick fix!

### JC2K — 2012-04-19T18:58:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/japanese-cold-training-and-the-aikido-connection.983/post-16626>

I always wondered if the sterotypical meditating under a waterfall stuff was "real" or not. Is that mentioned in their cold water training?

### MJ* — 2012-04-19T19:32:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/japanese-cold-training-and-the-aikido-connection.983/post-16641>

**Mei;16590 wrote:** I practice Ki Aikido, a non-martial art - developed in the 20th century in Japan from the more brutal Aikikai - that uses mastery and connection through ki (chi, qi, energy, the Force, whatever you want to call it) instead of physical strength to meet and overcome, or redirect, your opponent (or any energy attacking or obstructing you).

My father (also my sensei) has been listening to me yammer on about cold thermogenesis for the past month, and something finally clicked. He pulled out an old book to show me traditional Aikido cold water training, which the Ki society in Japan does every January, where everybody, including kids, goes out into freezing temperatures and has a dip in the ocean. Apparently, if you are supervised by ki-trained people, it ain't so bad. Sounds kind of familiar.

Tonight he said he found something else that said Tohei Sensei, the first teacher of our particular lineage, was a sickly boy until he began taking cold baths. He regained his health and then exceeded it, and, apparently, stumbled upon the Ancient Pathway. The dude was righteous in his day. [http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J04JI48GzPM&feature=related\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;J04JI48GzPM\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J04JI48GzPM&feature=related\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;J04JI48GzPM)

### Lahrie — 2012-04-19T20:08:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/japanese-cold-training-and-the-aikido-connection.983/post-16652>

> **Mei;16662 wrote:** stop thinking, and get in the tub.

Mei - this is my new mantra - do you mind if I add it to my signature?

I trained in Kempo for many years and I use the breathing technique\* when I put my back, shoulders and neck in the water. It releases tension and keeps me from holding my breath when the cold water hits.

\*Slow deep breath in through the nose (your chest and belly should expand). Hold for a second. Slow exhale through the mouth. Hold for a second. Repeat.

I was just reminded that during my Kenpo training, I was preparing for a very challenging exam, and I was given the advice to go to the ocean to center my Ki...I did, it was cold and exhilarating, but I remember feeling very good later, very balanced or something...calm, centered.

### Guest — 2012-04-19T20:43:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/japanese-cold-training-and-the-aikido-connection.983/post-16684>

> **Mei;16667 wrote:** Also, Suhenna: try to relax. Energy (and heat) can't flow if you're tensing up and resisting. If you notice a part of your body is full of tension (think: tight muscles, or if you're huddled or scrunched up), try to let it go. Breathe into it. We believe we need to fight against everything, we believe it so deep down that even our cells will fight something our mind considers "bad". Even if you can only relax for a second, that's enough. Next time you try, you might do a second and a half. You just slowly train your mind to find the feeling. It's the same when you're shivering, if you can concentrate for one moment to stop shivering. You'll start again, but many people have found they can do this. It's all good practice, but there's no quick fix!

Lots of wise stuff coming from such a young woman. I really love reading what you have to say.

Sometimes I can actually tap into some good breathing. Like the other evening I noticed my neck was tight and did do some breathing with the intention of relaxing my neck. It worked!!!--like a charm and I was amazed. What I don't understand is why I can't do this all the time. The next day I did some breathing and either I imagined something like smoke coming out or it was real --not sure which but it kind of took me back. Yet, I can't always get these results but will keep practicing without expecting anything from now on.

I will be looking for your posts from now on. Thanks, Mei for taking the time to post to me.

### Mei — 2012-04-19T21:21:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/japanese-cold-training-and-the-aikido-connection.983/post-16698>

@JC2K - Yes, it's real! I've come across it in my research...

@MJ\* - Tell him it's ultimate Aikido; it's hardcore Aikido; it's the very distilled essence of Aikido....  If he doesn't get it, he's practicing the wrong kind of Aikido... Oh, and I had the same experience! On Monday I arrived way too early for my soil science exam. The university is at the very end of Vancouver, surrounded by the sea with a designated nudie beach! So I ran down the hundreds of steps, stripped, jumped in and stood chest deep for half an hour, screeching and giggling every time the waves picked me up and tossed me at the shore (there was even an eagle circling! how much more perfect can you get??)... then I ran up barefoot up the hundreds of steps, high out of my mind, and back to class, where I got my brain back, put on my shoes and clothes and pretended I was normal. I didn't ace it, but I didn't sweat it either, and the exam was just a thing that happened, instead of something intense and awful. The swim, however, will be in my mind forever.

@Lahrie - Ahahaaha! Of course you can use it! Your Kempo breathing is almost the same as our Ki Breathing technique. We make the exhale loud, long, and sort of titrated, like an H sound. I hadn't even thought of using that in the water!! I'm going to do that, thanks for reminding me!

@Suhenna - sounds like you've got it, along with the non-expectation cherry on the cake!! We all have the gene for cold adaptation, we all have the ancient pathway burned in our brains, we can all visualize and create our realities, we can all be a co-ordinated part of this crazy beautiful world, we can all toss attackers over our hips when were 80 years old like it ain't no thang...we just have to learn how! Or, correction, we just have to do it.

AIKIDO QUOTE ALERT: "Do it first in your mind quickly, then relax completely, knowing it is done."

### PaleoDentist — 2012-04-21T07:00:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/japanese-cold-training-and-the-aikido-connection.983/post-17335>

mei, how does one learn this mind meditation?

### Dali Dula — 2012-04-21T15:44:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/japanese-cold-training-and-the-aikido-connection.983/post-17362>

I have been a aikido practitioner for over 20 yrs. I have been doing many types of breathing exercises while immersed. Misogi and also different yogic techniques. Fire breathing is really interesting. The nerve activation from the cold on the skin is interesting when you do breath work. You can really feel the breath throughout the body. I have also been having some deep philosophical realizations swimming in the cold. This is the ultimate yin/yang, cold/hot, dark/light. The cold is the part of our whole we have been missing. There are as many ways to breathe as there are breaths. It's all about mindfulness. Breathe and pay attention. Play with it. Breathe slow. Breathe fast. Exhale completely and hold till you have to breathe again. Inhale completely and hold until you must exhale. Breathe really fast in and out through the nose while pumping the belly. All these breathing techniques bring something different. They will all enhance your CT.

Terrible RT3 ratio, way low testosterone. This was two months prior to starting CT, but I had been eating dairy free low carb paleo as outlined in the Leptin Rx for five months at the time.

I'm scheduled to meet with the chief endo at the regional hospital, and maybe some of you have some additional points I'm missing that could be adressed. He thought my numbers were "interesting" and wanted some extra tests done. He suspected secondary pituitary failure (not sure if that's translated correctly)

Thinking and hoping CT might be able to get things sliding, but since I'm so royally messed up I'm not passing on seeing if I can tweak some on the warm adapted parameters too. I'm hoping maybe HCG or syntetic T3.

I don't know for sure, but from what I've found out it would be replacement testosterone by shots. That has side effects though; infertility is not uncommon since the testes stop making testosterone naturally. If I start that, there is no turning back. Not so great when we want to have kids.

HCG could trigger a natural increase in sex hormones, and would not cause any permanent problems - so I'm kind of hoping to start with that.

CT has also increased libido \_a lot\_ so I'm hoping whatever I'm dealing with is reversible and I can have access to my brains again

> **primat;26005 wrote: New results after six weeks of immersion shows a free testosterone index of about the same, but FSH, LH and testosterone (and SHBG) was lower. I wonder how much a sugar binge two days before would skew the results.
