# Thread: Increased Coconut Oil = Increased Appetite?!?!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/increased-coconut-oil-increased-appetite.1000/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-04-20T02:27:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/increased-coconut-oil-increased-appetite.1000/post-16806>

I have been absolutely ravenous the last 2 days . . . eating more in 2 days than I probably have the last week! The only change I've made is increasing my coconut oil consumption. Could that possibly cause an increase in appetite? I feel like I'm starving and I'm determined to never think about calories again, so I'm eating as much as I want (fortunately I can still easily go 5 hours b/w meals, so no snacking) at meals, but I end up nauseous a short while later because the meals are so HUGE! I'm hoping this doesn't cause a massive weight gain.

I was eating at least a tablespoon of tallow and about a half tablespoon coconut oil with each meal - I decided to flip that around and have more CO, but I'm wondering if that was a mistake!

### vkiernan — 2012-04-20T05:38:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/increased-coconut-oil-increased-appetite.1000/post-16901>

> **BJK77;16876 wrote:** I have been absolutely ravenous the last 2 days . . . eating more in 2 days than I probably have the last week! The only change I've made is increasing my coconut oil consumption. Could that possibly cause an increase in appetite? I feel like I'm starving and I'm determined to never think about calories again, so I'm eating as much as I want (fortunately I can still easily go 5 hours b/w meals, so no snacking) at meals, but I end up nauseous a short while later because the meals are so HUGE! e'm hoping this doesn't cause a massive weight gain.
>
>
>
> I was eating at least a tablespoon of tallow and about a half tablespoon coconut oil with each meal - I decided to flip that around and have more CO, but I'm wondering if that was a mistake!

Are you sure you are nausea from the size of the meal and not like low stomach acid or something? This might also be causing the hunger. I am about 3 lbs. heavier than you, your height and almost 50 with bad hormones to boot. My hunger goes up and down like a rollercoaster especially since CT'ing. I eat about 3 T of coconut oil for breakfast, probably another 2-3T at lunch and a little at dinner. No weight gain here. I love the stuff now and make sure I eat it to keep me full. I put it in my morning coffee, eggs, tea, bark, and anything else I can come up with. I also have butter with BAB too. I too eat as much as I want and sometimes get too full and uncomfortable but sometimes it doesn't hit me until too late (I also eat fast sometimes). I wouldn't worry as long as you eating according to the protocol you are following or unless you start gaining weight then either CT more or back off on it.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-20T09:01:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/increased-coconut-oil-increased-appetite.1000/post-16931>

About a month into CT my hunger went through the roof. Dr K said it was likely estrogen detox and to up the protein. It lasted about a week then receded. Not saying its not the CO in your case, but CT does seem an unpredictable variable.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-20T14:39:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/increased-coconut-oil-increased-appetite.1000/post-16955>

KIwi has the answer....posted above

### Glamorama — 2012-04-20T14:46:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/increased-coconut-oil-increased-appetite.1000/post-16958>

I suffered intense hunger for the first 5 weeks of CT, even after excess estrogen was dealt with through supplement. Then one day, the hunger was replaced with constant fullness. Now I easily IF and don't really feel like eating much at all.

### nzgal — 2012-04-20T15:21:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/increased-coconut-oil-increased-appetite.1000/post-16976>

Thanks!!! I'm gonna try putting a tblsp of coconut oil in my coffee. I have been dealing with hunger also!!!

### BJK77 — 2012-04-20T16:26:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/increased-coconut-oil-increased-appetite.1000/post-17027>

Thanks for all the responses. I wondered about detox, but I don't really have any other detox issues like I've had in the past . . . except for the fact that I'm over a month late for my TOM now. I'll just go with the hunger for now and hope it dissipates soon before I break the bank! It's crazy how much food I'm putting away right now : )

### Souldanzer — 2012-04-20T19:46:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/increased-coconut-oil-increased-appetite.1000/post-17136>

As a fellow eat-til-I-feel-sick I'm wondering... despite all the eating from CT, detox or whatever... if we're LS we shouldn't gain weight from it, correct?

Btw, the eating to the point of sickness has stopped for me. YAY. Haven't been sick or felt sick from eating for over 2 weeks now. But wow, it lasted for a while after starting CT. I still eat too much.

# Thread: Leptin and Thyroid, article by Dr. Ken Holtorf

### optimus prime — 2012-04-20T02:40:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-and-thyroid-article-by-dr-ken-holtorf.1001/post-16820>

Hi everyone!

I'm sure many of you have heard of Dr. Ken Holtorf, the founder of the National Academy of Hypothyroidism ([nahypothyroidism.org](http://http://nahypothyroidism.org/)). Well, I just got my labs back and they show a very low TSH, low range ft3 and ft4, and almost above range ft3. My ft3:rt3 ratio is 8.4. I spent some time on the national academy site and on [stopthethyroidmadness.com](http://http://stopthethyroidmadness.com) and today my efforts were rewarded. I found this article about how/why leptin resistant hypothyroid sufferers are most often overlooked by doctors. This is a must read if you have a hypothyroid symptoms and especially if you have low TSH with high rt3.

Dr Holtorf also recommends a serum leptin test to help diagnose. "Thus, if the leptin level is above 10, the TSH is unreliable (artificially decreased) and a normal TSH cannot be used to rule-out significant cellular hypothyroidism."

Here is the article (scroll down past the photo)! Please share your thoughts! [http://www.hormoneandlongevitycenter.com/leptinweightloss/](http://http://www.hormoneandlongevitycenter.com/leptinweightloss/)

Is this old news? If yes, not sure how I missed it. I am printing this out and handing it to my doctor and asking for T3.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-20T03:04:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-and-thyroid-article-by-dr-ken-holtorf.1001/post-16833>

# Thread: they can't find anything bad about resveratrol, but they are looking (good, I guess)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/they-cant-find-anything-bad-about-resveratrol-but-they-are-looking-good-i-guess.1003/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-04-20T03:06:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/they-cant-find-anything-bad-about-resveratrol-but-they-are-looking-good-i-guess.1003/post-16834>

[http://www.winespectator.com/webfeature/show/id/46402](http://http://www.winespectator.com/webfeature/show/id/46402) Posted: February 15, 2012

Understanding How Resveratrol Works

Studies have shown the red-wine chemical has health effects; a new study tries to find out why

**Chung, who worked with researchers internationally, as well as biochemists and pharmacists at American universities, found that resveratrol acts not just as an anti-inflammatory substance, but also as a genetic manipulator—all to the benefit of the lab rodents—through both direct and indirect chemical pathways.

On the positive side, the researchers found that resveratrol blocks the action of a muscle enzyme called phosphodiesterase 4 (PDE4), which causes cell degradation. Resveratrol's ability to inhibit PDE4 is likely responsible for the anti-inflammatory observations in the mice, the study notes. A PDE4 inhibitor drug based on resveratrol could have the potential to fight several illnesses, though that is very hypothetical at this stage.

Resveratrol also appears to activate sirtuins, a family of proteins believed to regulate genetic activity, repair DNA and extend lifespan, though scientists are not sure of sirtuins' impact yet.**

[http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/02/120202151133.htm](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/02/120202151133.htm)

The wine industry is very interested.

### chocolate — 2012-04-20T18:18:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/they-cant-find-anything-bad-about-resveratrol-but-they-are-looking-good-i-guess.1003/post-17111>

[http://findarticles.com/p/articles/mi\_m0FDN/is\_3\_15/ai\_n55833626/](http://http://findarticles.com/p/articles/mi_m0FDN/is_3_15/ai_n55833626/)

FindArticles / Health / Alternative Medicine Review / Sept, 2010

A review of the sirtuin system, its clinical implications, and the potential role of dietary activators like resveratrol: Part 1

Abstract

The silent information regulator (SIR) genes (sirtuins) comprise a highly conserved family of proteins, with one or more sirtuins present in virtually all species from bacteria to mammals. In mammals seven sirtuin genes--SIRT1 to SIRT7--have been identified. Emerging from research on the sirtuins is a growing appreciation that the sirtuins are a very complicated biological response system that influences many other regulator molecules and pathways in complex manners. Responses of this system to environmental factors, as well as its role in health and disease, are currently incompletely characterized and at most partially understood. **This article reviews the mammalian sirtuin system, discusses the dietary, lifestyle, and environmental factors that influence sirtuin activity, and summarizes research on the importance of vitamin B3 in supporting sirtuin enzyme activity, as well as the role specifically of the amide form of this vitamin--nicotinamide--to inhibit sirtuin enzyme activity. Polyphenols, especially resveratrol, influence sirtuins. Existing evidence on these nutritional compounds, as they relate to the sirtuin system, is reviewed. In Part 2 of this review, clinical situations where sirtuins might play a significant role, including longevity, obesity, fatty liver disease, cardiovascular health, neurological disease, and cancer, are discussed. (Altern Med Rev 2010;15 (3):245-263)

What had me afraid of the Vitamin B3?**

### AKMan — 2012-04-20T18:34:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/they-cant-find-anything-bad-about-resveratrol-but-they-are-looking-good-i-guess.1003/post-17118>

Great thread, chocolate! I don't drink much wine, but I do eat a ton of dark chocolate. I eat 1 oz, at least, of 100% Baker's chocolate every night when I am done with dinner.

I bought a bottle of resveratrol capsules, but read later it was not the same as getting res from natural sources, due to a missing confounder.

Since chocolate has resveratrol, maybe it also has the confounder, so I now take my resveratrol capsules just before I eat my square of chocolate. Sounds reasonable, eh?

### chocolate — 2012-04-20T20:33:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/they-cant-find-anything-bad-about-resveratrol-but-they-are-looking-good-i-guess.1003/post-17159>

I am trying to find if apple cider vinegar is a sirtuin.

[http://www.landesbioscience.com/curie/chapter/4934/](http://http://www.landesbioscience.com/curie/chapter/4934/)

Chapter Category: Cell Biology

From the book Sensing in Nature

Involvement of Sirtuins in Life‑Span and Aging Related Diseases

Ulrich Mahlknecht and Barbara Zschoernig

Since healthy aging remains one of the ideals of modern society, both, the identification of the underlying molecular mechanisms and interventions regarding the aging process are of considerable interest. **Among the mechanisms currently being considered, the sirtuin family of histone deacetylases have been implicated to play a crucial role during the aging process both due to their requirement of NAD+ as a cofactor for enzymatic activity, which determines a crucial link between sirtuins and the energy dependent regulation of gene transcription and their versatile target substrates mainly consisting of key regulators of metabolic, stress and cell cycle control. This chapter summarizes current evidences linking sirtuins to aging and outlines their potential as promising therapeutic targets for the treatment of age‑related diseases.**

ISBN: 978-1-4614-1703-3

Publication date: January 30, 2012

Series: Special Books

There are lots of search clues in the breakdown of the book.

### chocolate — 2012-04-20T20:44:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/they-cant-find-anything-bad-about-resveratrol-but-they-are-looking-good-i-guess.1003/post-17162>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2396790/](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2396790/) Sirtuins: a conserved key unlocking AceCS activity

**In mammals, acetate is derived from both exogenous sources (e.g. bacterial fermentation in the gut and dietary sources such as vinegar) and endogenous sources (e.g. ethanol metabolism, conversion of acetyl-CoA into acetate by acetyl-CoA hydrolase, hydrolysis of acetylcholine by acetylcholinesterases in the nervous system, and deacetylation of lysine residues by protein deacetylases) \[5,8\]. Acetate can be converted to acetyl-CoA either in the cytoplasm by AceCS1 or in the mitochondria by AceCS2 \[9,10\] (Figure 1). Acetyl-CoA is used in the synthesis of fatty acids, some amino acids and ketone bodies, and for entry of carbons into the TCA cycle.

A conserved metabolic switch

Two recent papers demonstrate that the mode of AceCS regulation discovered in Salmonella is conserved in mammals** \[6,7\]. Both studies show that AceCS2 is activated when it is deacetylated by SIRT3 (a mitochondrial sirtuin) \[6,7\]. Denu and colleagues \[7\] have further elucidated this conserved mechanism by demonstrating that SIRT1 activates cytoplasmic AceCS1 by deacetylation (Figure 1).

I find the vinegar is good section, I am wondering if the salmonella switch conserved is the Factor X penguin connection and why some of us look like we have beaks?

# Thread: Party meal ideas??
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/party-meal-ideas.1008/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-04-20T16:58:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/party-meal-ideas.1008/post-17051>

I'm having 2 parties next weekend for my daughter's birthday - one with the family and one with her friends. My kids have been doing so well eating a paleo diet and I really want to stick to it even at the parties. I have no problem cooking a big paleo meal for everyone, whether they like it or not , BUT I can't exactly go all out due to $$. Not to mention, her friends are used to pop tarts for breakfast and pizza for dinner so I need to at least find something they'll eat.

Any suggestions? I'll need dinner ideas for the family - who are picky and used to bland, PA Dutch type foods. Blech! For her friends, I need dinner and breakfast ideas. I was considering a taco bar / baked sweet potato bar where I could just make a huge pot of chili and put out toppings for everyone. Looking for other ideas though too. For breakfast, I'm clueless right now. I know most of the kids won't eat eggs. Bacon would go over well, but what else? I've tried pancakes and other baked goods with coconut/almond flour for them before, but they didn't go over well. My kids love them, thank God, but the others not so much.

### AKMan — 2012-04-20T18:22:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/party-meal-ideas.1008/post-17113>

Breakfast - fruit platters; get a couple melons, grapes, berries cut into bite-size pieces and and serve buffet style.

Lunch - Primalized Meatballs, lunchmeat (provide bread for the losers), fruit&veggie platters w/cheese slices

Dinner - Stuffed peppers, meatloaf, roasted chicken or turkey, ham, roast beef. Provide potatoes/rice for the naysayers.

### BJK77 — 2012-04-20T19:39:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/party-meal-ideas.1008/post-17133>

Thanks for the ideas AKMan. Fruit would probably go over well with the kids. I just worry it isn't very filling, but I'll load them up on some bacon too

As far as the bread for losers, I absolutely refuse to have the junk in my house, so that's a no-go! My son and I have a gluten intolerance (possibly even celiac), so no gluten enters this place at all. That's why I was so stuck on meals - we used to always grill burgers/hot dogs or do lunch meats. We manage fine without buns/bread, but I know the "losers" would not.

### Souldanzer — 2012-04-20T20:19:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/party-meal-ideas.1008/post-17152>

Do you own the "Nourishing Traditions" cookbook... I bet there would be some great ideas in there.

Bacon Pizza? It looks delicious but I'd eat the whole thing in one sitting if I made it, lol.

Fermented Soda type beverages (ginger ale, etc) for the kids? Served in pop-top beer bottles just for the fun of it

A nutty cereal like topping for the fruit with whipping cream/coconut cream? And a chocolate sauce... yum

Guacamole!

I would make sure to have a protein option with breakfast... since we all know what happens if it doesn't! Unless they leave after breakfast, that is.... lol.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-20T21:08:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/party-meal-ideas.1008/post-17165>

you could use nut crackers instead of bread, so your son won't get hurt if he eats them, they are gluten free. And how bout grilled chicken wings, I know chicken isn't the best thing but they are good for low carb party food. also how bout shrimp & cocktail sauce? Guacamole & plantain chips?

I am going to a large party for memorial weekend, you have me thinking about how I am going to handle that since I am not the hostess! I may have to bring my own food, that's a tad bit weird.

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-04-21T00:37:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/party-meal-ideas.1008/post-17234>

I had a birthday party with a taco bar that went over really well -- just serve all the fixings in bowls and let folks make taco salads. Super easy.

Meatballs work great, too! I use this meatball grill basket when I'm entertaining. [http://reviews.williams-sonoma.com/3177/meatball-grill-basket/reviews.htm](http://http://reviews.williams-sonoma.com/3177/meatball-grill-basket/reviews.htm)

I would go with omelets/egg muffins for breakfast. You might be surprised how creative the kids get if they can add their own toppings to their egg creations.

Maybe have lots of finger foods -- fruits and veggies cut in fun shapes

Soups work great, too! I like the idea of a sweet potato bar. Fun!

### Justfourme — 2012-04-23T04:54:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/party-meal-ideas.1008/post-17967>

Are you eating any dairy or no? I have made a "pizza crust" in the past that uses shredded cheeses and cream cheese and some spices. You could make individual size pizzas so the kids can add the toppings they like. Or use meats and cheeses, cut up veggies and fruits to make "faces". For example slices of meat for the face, banana slices with a blueberry in the center for eyes, shredded carrots for hair, an apple slice for a mouth or ears, etc. set rules that they have to eat what they use. Let them use their imaginations. Take pictures of their creations, then have them chow down! Food for kids can be healthy and fun!

### Justfourme — 2012-04-23T05:02:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/party-meal-ideas.1008/post-17969>

Since you mentioned most of the kids don't care for eggs the egg muffins prob won't work but you could have some for those who do like eggs. Maybe cook some sausage links ahead of time, then wrap with bacon, secure with toothpicks and broil? serve with fruit kabobs. Kids love things on toothpicks or kabobs!

### Justfourme — 2012-04-23T05:06:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/party-meal-ideas.1008/post-17971>

I have also made the cheese pizza crusts in rounds about the size of a hamb bun and used them as a top and bottom "bun" for burgers.

### Glamorama — 2012-04-23T05:09:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/party-meal-ideas.1008/post-17973>

I'd do a sausage sizzle with gluten-free sausages, kids love that, You could make your own ketchup to go with it, its super-easy. Instead of buns or bread, just provide big bowls of leafy salad as a side? Then there is platter-options like cold cut meats, sun-dried tomatoes, cheese cubes, olives...

### BJK77 — 2012-04-26T18:17:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/party-meal-ideas.1008/post-19048>

Thanks so much for all the suggestions. I decided on the following menu:

\- meatballs

\- baked sweet potatoes

\- broccoli salad

\- glazed carrots (requested by my daughter! )

\- deviled eggs

\- fruit salad

\- tossed salads

For breakfast for the kiddos we'll do bacon and/or sausage hash, fruit salad and egg muffins for those who like them. Hopefully everyone will be happy and I won't kill myself trying to please them all!

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-26T20:11:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/party-meal-ideas.1008/post-19088>

Bacon "sushi", Bacon with a little salmon on top.

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-04-26T23:43:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/party-meal-ideas.1008/post-19144>

BK, sounds like a great menu! What is your recipe for broccoli salad?

# Thread: Would someone please summarize Dr. K's thoughts on hCG?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-someone-please-summarize-dr-ks-thoughts-on-hcg.1009/>
**Subforum:** HCG Protocol
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### curves64 — 2012-04-20T19:45:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-someone-please-summarize-dr-ks-thoughts-on-hcg.1009/post-17135>

Hi Everyone. I'm super thankful for Dr. K, this thread and this forum. I'm wondering if someone can summarize Dr. K's thoughts on hCG. This is what I believe he thinks on the topic:

\-A round of Hcg is one way to reset Lepin sensitivity

\-He prefers the non-homeopathic hcg to the homeopathic version (not sure if that is even an issue anymore)

\-When we become Leptin sensitive, we should no longer do hcg

\-Cold thermo is an excellent complement to hcg

\-One you finish your round you need to begin a paleo/primal diet within 6-8 weeks

\-You should not add coconut oil to the hcg protocol

Did I get this right? Am I missing anything? Thank you so much for your help with this!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-20T19:52:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-someone-please-summarize-dr-ks-thoughts-on-hcg.1009/post-17139>

Dr. Kruse like his Leptin Reset and CT over Hcg.......how is that?

# Thread: getting labs done
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-labs-done.1011/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### sisterclare — 2012-04-20T22:51:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-labs-done.1011/post-17191>

What do you do when your doctor takes forever to have you get your labs done? I have asked for his help, and he certainly wants to but, after two weeks, still hasn't written up the order form for me to get them done. I really don't have any other doctor to go to. I have called his office and his nurse says that she will call and talk to me about this, but it doesn't happen. I do have insurance and know that some of these tests will not be covered, but I would like to get a roll on this so I can go ahead with my life.

### Souldanzer — 2012-04-20T23:36:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-labs-done.1011/post-17205>

Two options:

\- you go online and order the tests yourself (no insurance)

\- you keep calling your doc's office every.single.day. and re-state your request. You become a PIA. If they still don't react you come up with a symptom and tell them that it's getting worse. Heart palpitations work great for this. Urgent enough to get them to take action, easy to blame on anxiety if everything comes back fine...

There's another option and that's get a different doc (which would be my choice) but it sounds like that one's out.

# Thread: Thyroid results
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-results.1014/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Destiny — 2012-04-20T23:55:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-results.1014/post-17213>

My doctor told me that TSH is a bit higher but the rest is OK. Was she correct?

TSH - 4.290 0.270-4.200 uIU/ml

T4 - 9.6 4.5-12.0 ug/dl

FT4 - 1.27 0.83-1.62 ng/dl

Free T4 Index 3.3 1.5 -3.8

T3 Total 86 80-200ng/dl

T3 Uptake 34.1 24.3- 39.0 %

I asked for rT3 but I don't think the doctor knew what it was since she told me that she always runs the total thyroid panel.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-21T00:06:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-results.1014/post-17222>

Not in my humble albeit direct opinion. The FT3 is the available hormone in your body that gets the "work" done. Without it the results above don't tell you much. The FT3 should be near the top of the range. The Total T3 doesn't tell you how much is actually FREE and able to be used by the body. But you are in the very low end of TT3 so I would go back to the doc and get that FT3 tested. The FT4 should be mid to mid-high range. You are right there now. Lastly, there is the virtually useless TSH test. With that said the 4.29 is way to high to be optimal. There is an endocrinologists group that states anything over 3.0 is hypo (can remember what organization but should be searchable online if you need a reference for the doc). Go back to the doc and get FT3 and rT3 tested. They need to be tested at the same time anyway to get an accurate measure of the ratio of FT3 to rT3. I bet you're fatigued, sluggish, maybe have trouble loosing weight, possible hair issues (like the outer corner of your eyebrows are disappearing or hair falling out). I'd say you are showing hypo numbers but you need the other tests!

You probably also should get your Vit D, iron panel, and adrenal salivary cortisol tested. These go hand-in-hand with hypothyrodism. My guess is your insurance would pay for all those but maybe not the adrenal test.

### Destiny — 2012-04-21T00:15:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-results.1014/post-17228>

> **Patty Cakes;17292 wrote:** Not in my humble albeit direct opinion. The FT3 is the available hormone in your body that gets the "work" done. Without it the results above don't tell you much. The FT3 should be near the top of the range. The Total T3 doesn't tell you how much is actually FREE and able to be used by the body. But you are in the very low end of TT3 so I would go back to the doc and get that FT3 tested. The FT4 should be mid to mid-high range. You are right there now. Lastly, there is the virtually useless TSH test. With that said the 4.29 is way to high to be optimal. There is an endocrinologists group that states anything over 3.0 is hypo (can remember what organization but should be searchable online if you need a reference for the doc). Go back to the doc and get FT3 and rT3 tested. They need to be tested at the same time anyway to get an accurate measure of the ratio of FT3 to rT3. I bet you're fatigued, sluggish, maybe have trouble loosing weight, possible hair issues (like the outer corner of your eyebrows are disappearing or hair falling out). I'd say you are showing hypo numbers but you need the other tests!
>
>
>
> You probably also should get your Vit D, iron panel, and adrenal salivary cortisol tested. These go hand-in-hand with hypothyrodism. My guess is your insurance would pay for all those but maybe not the adrenal test.

Thanks, Pat, for the interpretation. I forgot to mention that I have been using Iodoral for the past 5 months. Could that be a part of the problem?

You are right with the issues above. The only one that I don't have is the hair loss. My vitamin D level is 114. My salivary cortisol was high at night. I asked for the iron panel but probably did not get it. My hematology result page is full of abbreviations.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-21T00:31:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-results.1014/post-17233>

Ioderal, from what I know should help those lab results show better functioning. I forgot to mention, if you haven't already had them, get thyroid antibody tests (TgAb, TPO) to check for Hashimotos.

Resource to learn more: [www.stopthethyroidmadness.com](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com)

CTing has really helped my energy level so that may help too. It took a month or so of doing it for me to notice a difference.

If you started supplementing without Selenium it could be a problem, especially at high doses. What was yoru WBC? If it's low normal, I'd present that to your doc and ask for the Rev T3 testing -- autoimmune hypothyroidism goes hand in hand with low WBCs and docs at least understand that part.

mambo, my WBC is 4.29 3.40-11.80 x10(3)/ul - I think I am low normal as you said. I hope you are not overestimating my doc!

I was taking Selenium, zinc and magnesium when I started to take 1/2 Iodoral (6mg) a day. I never took more than that. Should I stop it completely?

### mambo — 2012-04-30T03:08:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-results.1014/post-19976>

4.3 is exactly what you'd expect for Hashi. And if you have Hashi, get ANA tested: tell your doc, "Look my WBC is low, as is typical for autoimmune sufferers of hypothyroidism (i.e., Hashimoto's). Please test my TG and TPO, and Reverse T3 for confirmation. While at it, since Hashi also overlaps with autoimmne conditions, please test my Antinuclear Antibodies (ANA) to see if I'm positive." If doc says no, I would just go straight to an endocrinologist (for Hashi tests) and rheumatologist (for ANA).

### Sem — 2012-04-30T05:40:03+0300

### mambo — 2012-04-30T06:41:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-results.1014/post-20015>

I hope u were taking about 200 mcg Se but didn't start immediately at 6mg for I. You have build youself up, not increasing too fast, otherwise you could exacerbate your thyroid. Same with when weaning. Also, go easy on Zn as you can overdose on it, too: no more than 30-50mg daily. Mg is ok and I think Dr. K recommends up to 1200mg; when you have the runs, you'll know with Mg.

# Thread: Cool Party Trick
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cool-party-trick.1016/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-04-21T00:13:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cool-party-trick.1016/post-17226>

Tell people water can be both hot and cold at the same time.

Get three big plastic cups. Fill one with hot tap water, one with cold tap water (with ice), and the other with room temperature water.

Put 2 fingers of one hand in the hot, and the other hand in the cold. Leave for a few minutes.

Now put all your fingers in the room temp water. Freaky!

### MJ* — 2012-04-21T01:55:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cool-party-trick.1016/post-17263>

>

>
>
>
> Put 2 fingers of one hand in the hot, and the other hand in the cold. Leave for a few minutes.
>
>
>
> Now put all your fingers in the room temp water. Freaky!

That's cool but do it one at a time or there might be a long line for the bathroom!

# Thread: Rotavirus (gluten is mimicker) and Blood Type A
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rotavirus-gluten-is-mimicker-and-blood-type-a.1018/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-04-21T00:40:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rotavirus-gluten-is-mimicker-and-blood-type-a.1018/post-17236>

[http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/04/120415151332.htm](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/04/120415151332.htm)

Surprise finding

"We wondered how this genotype of rotavirus recognized a cellular glycan," said Prasad. "With colleagues at Emory (University School of Medicine), we did a glycan array analysis to see which glycans interacted with the top of the virus spike (called VP8\*)."

The only type of glycan that interacted with VP8\* was type A histo-blood group antigen, he said.

"That was surprising," he said. "We thought it had to be a glycan with sialic acid."

The histo-blood group antigen A does not have sialic acid.

However, when Dr. Liya Hu, a post-doctoral researcher in Prasad's laboratory, determined the structure of the VP8\* domain, she found that **the type A glycan bound to the rotavirus spike protein at the same place as the sialic acid would have in an animal rotavirus.** Histo-blood group antigens are known to promote binding of norovirus and Helicobacter pylori cells to intestinal cells, but this had never been demonstrated in rotavirus.

### chocolate — 2012-04-21T03:35:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rotavirus-gluten-is-mimicker-and-blood-type-a.1018/post-17299>

[http://www.labspaces.net/116144/Legumes\_give\_nitrogen\_supplying\_bacteria\_special\_access\_pass](http://http://www.labspaces.net/116144/Legumes_give_nitrogen_supplying_bacteria_special_access_pass)

All Health Bioscience Physical Science Environment Space Tech Life Origins Misc

Press Release

Legumes give nitrogen-supplying bacteria special access pass

Tuesday, December 20, 2011

A 125-year debate on how nitrogen-fixing bacteria are able to breach the cell walls of legumes has been settled. A paper to be published on Monday by John Innes Centre scientists reports that plants themselves allow bacteria in.

Once inside the right cells, these bacteria take nitrogen from the air and supply it to legumes in a form they can use, ammonia. Whether the bacteria breach the cell walls by producing enzymes that degrade it, or the plant does the work for them, has been contested since an 1887 paper in which the importance of the breach was first recognised.

**"Our results are so clear we can unequivocally say that the plant supplies enzymes to break down its own cell walls and allow bacteria access," said Professor Allan Downie, lead author from the John Innes Centre, which is strategically funded by BBSRC**

### janagram — 2012-04-21T03:54:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rotavirus-gluten-is-mimicker-and-blood-type-a.1018/post-17305>

chocolate, what does the first article mean to you? In layman's terms. Cuz I'm interested.

### chocolate — 2012-04-21T04:16:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rotavirus-gluten-is-mimicker-and-blood-type-a.1018/post-17311>

I think it means that a special (sugar) glycan A type only, accommodates the rotavirus. If I had to WAG, and really that's all it would be, we adapted to allow the virus in. The virus has to do something for us. We give up resources, so it has to be mutual benefit. Plus, we consummate, it is automatic? I don't know if that glycan is there all the time. I know the lacto keep the gates closed, probably all winter. And lacto is a treatment for rotovirus. But sugars open the gate.....inside or out.

### chocolate — 2012-04-21T05:29:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rotavirus-gluten-is-mimicker-and-blood-type-a.1018/post-17320>

I think it would have to be in a dish of each kind of gut bacteria, lacto and e choli. Then see what happens. and both temperatures. The other blood types seem to accept it regardless. A's solicit.

### chocolate — 2012-04-22T00:24:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rotavirus-gluten-is-mimicker-and-blood-type-a.1018/post-17561>

[http://www.glycoforum.gr.jp/science/word/evolution/ES-A03E.html](http://http://www.glycoforum.gr.jp/science/word/evolution/ES-A03E.html)

Lectins that make sialic acid more diverse

Lectins that recognize sialic acids are categorized into three groups, pathogenic lectins towards glycoconjugates from vertebrates, endogenous lectins in vertebrates, and exogenous lectins from other organisms such as insects and plants. Plants have lectins that recognize sialic acids and the plant lectins are powerful tools for the detection of sialic acids. **Because plant itself does not have sialic acids, plant lectins are considered to function in protecting itself from foreign enemies that have sialoglycoconjugates. Alternatively, the real ligands for these lectins may be mimetic molecules different from sialic acids.

The binding specificities of these lectins are different according to the sialic acid species and linkages, and may be the result of frequent encounters with endogenous lectins. In brief, the more diverse the sialic acid structure becomes by species, substitution of the hydroxyl group with sulfation, or methylation by adding more sialic acids toward sialic acids and by changing the structure of inner glycan chains, the more liable the endogenous lectins are to be multifunctional and beneficial to the organism.**

### chocolate — 2012-04-22T00:59:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rotavirus-gluten-is-mimicker-and-blood-type-a.1018/post-17574>

[http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2010/12/101222205346.htm](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2010/12/101222205346.htm)

Designer Probiotics Could Reduce Obesity

The same group of researchers previously found that microbially produced CLA was able to reduce the viability of colon cancer cells by 92%. "It is possible that a CLA-producing probiotic may also be able to keep colon cancer cells in check. **All our findings to date demonstrate that the metabolism of gut bacteria can modulate host cell activity in ways that are beneficial to the host," explained Dr Stanton. "We need to further investigate the effects of CLA-producing bacteria on human metabolism, but our work so far certainly opens up new possibilities for the use of probiotics for improvement of human health."**

# Thread: Dr Kruse's Creamy Coconut Candy
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruses-creamy-coconut-candy.1019/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-04-21T00:52:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruses-creamy-coconut-candy.1019/post-17240>

Hi Friends,

I just wanted to say a little thank you to Dr Kruse for sharing all his discoveries. This recipe is great for anyone but even children love it. It's the kind of "candy" I want my children to eat! I like adding a few raisins to the mix. A small number of raisins gives a sweet hit without increasing carbohydrate load very much. This candy will not kick me out of ketosis though the same amount of raw cheese will. I hope you enjoy it:

[http://www.eatkamloops.org/dr-kruses-creamy-coconut-candy/](http://http://www.eatkamloops.org/dr-kruses-creamy-coconut-candy/)

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-04-21T03:56:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruses-creamy-coconut-candy.1019/post-17306>

### Glamorama — 2012-04-21T10:09:07+0300

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-04-21T17:21:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruses-creamy-coconut-candy.1019/post-17421>

> **janagram;17323 wrote:** how do you know you are kicked out of ketosis...?
>
>
>
> AND...what is coconut cream? I have coconut manna....?

I use Ketostix to tell if I am in ketosis.

Coconut cream is very finely ground coconut meat. Coconut manna, I think, is the same as coconut cream. You can find different "grades" of ground coconut. Some are more "creamy" then others. I only recommended the brand in my post because you can get it in my area. Please find a similar local supplier.

# Thread: Mucous is the first antibacterial- Mucous in a petri dish and penicillin
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mucous-is-the-first-antibacterial-mucous-in-a-petri-dish-and-penicillin.1020/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-04-21T02:14:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mucous-is-the-first-antibacterial-mucous-in-a-petri-dish-and-penicillin.1020/post-17266>

[http://www.rcsb.org/pdb/101/motm.do?momID=9](http://http://www.rcsb.org/pdb/101/motm.do?momID=9)

The First Antibiotic

Attacking Bacteria

Lysozyme protects us from the ever-present danger of bacterial infection. It is a small enzyme that attacks the protective cell walls of bacteria. **Bacteria build a tough skin of carbohydrate chains, interlocked by short peptide strands, that braces their delicate membrane against the cell's high osmotic pressure. Lysozyme breaks these carbohydrate chains, destroying the structural integrity of the cell wall. The bacteria burst under their own internal pressure.

Alexander Fleming discovered lysozyme during a deliberate search for medical antibiotics. Over a period of years, he added everything that he could think of to bacterial cultures, looking for anything that would slow their growth. He discovered lysozyme by chance. **One day, when he had a cold, he added a drop of mucus to the culture and, much to his surprise, it killed the bacteria. He had discovered one of our own natural defenses against infection. Unfortunately, lysozyme is a larg**e molecule that is not particularly useful as a drug. It can be applied topically, but cannot rid the entire body of disease, because it is too large to travel between cells. Fortunately, Fleming continued his search, finding a true antibiotic drug five years later: penicillin.

A Cellular Guardian

Lysozyme protects many places that are rich in potential food for bacterial growth. The lysozyme pictured here is from hen egg whites, where it serves to protect the proteins and fats that will nourish the developing chick. It was the first enzyme ever to have its structure solved--you can find it in PDB entry 2lyz. Our tears and mucus contain lysozyme to resist infection of our exposed surfaces. Our blood is the worst place to have bacteria grow, as they would be delivered to all corners of the body**. In the blood, lysozyme provides some protection, along with the more powerful methods employed by the immune system.

SAFE Starches?


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Thread: Are fermented foods considered snacks?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-fermented-foods-considered-snacks.1023/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-04-21T03:44:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-fermented-foods-considered-snacks.1023/post-17300>

I assume this is a 'yes,' but I was wondering because I've always liked to consume ferments at different times for maximum benefit. My understanding has always been that if they are eaten with meals you really don't benefit much from the probiotics, but more from the enzymes that will help to digest your meal. However, taken in between meals I've been told is when we get the most out of the probiotics.

I'm thinking of sauerkraut, coconut kefir, the Kevita drinks, etc.

### Souldanzer — 2012-04-21T03:58:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-fermented-foods-considered-snacks.1023/post-17308>

I've been toying with the Kevita drinks that have like 2 carbs total per serving  Not a good idea.

I think the rule is anything that has calories must go.... plain tea it is.

### Glamorama — 2012-04-21T11:52:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-fermented-foods-considered-snacks.1023/post-17348>

### Souldanzer — 2012-04-25T19:05:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-fermented-foods-considered-snacks.1023/post-18764>

Kombucha still has caffeine and a bit of sugar, so I blamed my addiction on that. Kevita has none of that.... a gram or two of carbs and I don't even add stevia to my homemade kevita.

# Thread: What to do about a root canal...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-do-about-a-root-canal.1025/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 40
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jennie747 — 2012-04-21T15:40:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-do-about-a-root-canal.1025/post-17359>

I have a tooth that has probably had an undetected crack down in the root for 15+ years. It had a large filling and the tooth was breaking, so a crown was put on it about 15 years ago. Since then I have had pain periodically when I bite on something small and hard. About 5 years ago I had the crown replaced thinking maybe that was the problem. Same thing happened. Then they did a root canal on it about a year ago. Same thing still happens. They are now thinking it is cracked underneath. Nothing shows up on x-rays but the symptoms fit.

I now know root canals are bad & don't feel comfortable leaving it there, but don't want to open myself to worse problems by pulling it. None of the options for dealing with the empty space seem very appealing. The pain is very intermittent and I have already lived with it a very long time.

How bad are root canals really and would it be best just to leave it alone?

### PaulaRichards — 2012-04-22T19:35:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-do-about-a-root-canal.1025/post-17798>

+1! I'd love to hear some opinions on that too. I had a root canal a couple of years ago before I heard they weren't good. It has been tender at times since then. I didn't opt for the crown because it was still tender & I just had a feeling that the crown would make resolving any potential future problems worse.

### Inger — 2012-04-22T19:57:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-do-about-a-root-canal.1025/post-17807>

I have two teeths that are "empty". The dentist wanted to make a rootkanal cause it was infected and hurt so badly, pulled the root out and cleaned it and put a cement-filling on the hole until next appointement where the gutta-perka had to be filled in the canal. I never made another appointement. I am not thinking gutta perka is natural at all and don't want it in my teeth. So they are just empty. One teeth is about 6 years empty, and the other one year. I have no problems with the teehts when I eat right. I have no idea if this is really bad desicion but I saw no other option. I figure one day I might have to pull the teeths but that is okay. Until now they are fine.

### Souldanzer — 2012-04-22T21:52:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-do-about-a-root-canal.1025/post-17859>

> **paulalynn;17869 wrote:** +1! I'd love to hear some opinions on that too. I had a root canal a couple of years ago before I heard they weren't good. It has been tender at times since then. I didn't opt for the crown because it was still tender & I just had a feeling that the crown would make resolving any potential future problems worse.

FYI, teeth that have root canals need crowns to protect them as they get brittle over time. I had a friend crack off a tooth at the gum line b/c she decided not to have the crown done for financial reasons. Her bill increased exponentially now needing an implant (front incisor).

### Jennie747 — 2012-04-28T00:39:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-do-about-a-root-canal.1025/post-19463>

I had to get the root canal _through_ my pretty new porcelain crown. Now it looks like it has a filling! Bleh.

### primat — 2012-06-12T22:05:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-do-about-a-root-canal.1025/post-30735>

I'm kicking the old thread alive with me wondering

Long story short: I had to redo a root canal from a couple of years back due to increasing inflammation under one side of the tooth.

I got a prescription for pencillin to take if the inflammation flared up and got painful.

Due to me having a really slow immune system, I'm thinking that maybe I should take the 10 days antibiotics to reduce the risk of the infection not going. I can rebuild my gut flora after; but I don't think I have money for an apisectomy if the inflammation doesn't die. The dentist said it's best to let the body handle it - however, knowing my body, I'm not THAT sure :-D

I'm in for a followup in six weeks. (For a total of $860 bah...)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-13T02:39:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-do-about-a-root-canal.1025/post-30765>

Teeth are my specialty.......I dont talk about them much here but i do on some consults and people are amazed about what they tell us. You want to save a tooth if you can but you need to dictate what the tooth is filled with and how its done.......once you know the details you can shop the endodontist/dentist

### TerrierMom — 2012-06-13T06:47:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-do-about-a-root-canal.1025/post-30847>

I had a root canal a year ago. I do have a mouth full of fillings and crowns, no wisdom teeth (out 30 yrs ago). I do get recurrent sinus infections (once it led to pneumonia!) every December and May almost like clockwork which has me thinking allergies .... But I wonder with my mouth so close to sinuses, if something else ain't going on!!!

I will say my gums no longer bleed and within 2 months of the Leptin Rx my hygienist was AMAZED at my lovely healthy gums!! major improvement..... So something's going right!

This is also true of the post and cores present in the teeth and some of the porcelain fused to metal crowns........JanSz has spent 30-50K on his mouth too.......Typical in Polish immigrants. When I was in dental school I had a polish engineer from United Technologies who had so much work done I graduated a year early on my requirements.......He actually had more work than Jansz did.

### Joann — 2012-06-21T06:18:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-do-about-a-root-canal.1025/post-32625>

> **Fara;32698 wrote:** Vicki, did you have the same symptoms while you were in a temporary, or did the pain start after the permanent crown was cemented? Defintely DO NOT "Pull" your tooth.

since that happened to me I'm curious. fine with temporary, then felt awful with crown to the point took crown off and the dentist put a crown had no metal.

still felt not right ....that was 2 yrs ago. now I m with an infection.

### Fara — 2012-06-22T02:52:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-do-about-a-root-canal.1025/post-32862>

> **Joann;32747 wrote:** Fara, why you ask about symptoms if while with temporary or after crown?
>
> since that happened to me I'm curious. fine with temporary, then felt awful with crown to the point took crown off and the dentist put a crown had no metal.
>
> still felt not right ....that was 2 yrs ago. now I m with an infection.

Joann,

If the tooth is ok during the temp phase and sensitive with the perm crown, it could be due to the type off cement used, and it may just take a little time for the tooth to calm down. Or it may require something as small a bite adjustment as Dr. kruse mentioned.

But if the crown was seated two months ago and sensitive now, then it is very likely that Vicki will need a root canal. The nerve becomes inflammed and cannot heal. it is not all that uncommon for the tooth to be aymptomatic prior to the crown preparation, or after perm crown is cemented and all of a sudden months, or years later it starts acting up.

### vwaggs — 2012-06-22T05:42:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-do-about-a-root-canal.1025/post-32891>

Fara I have the crown because of the crack. It was actually in Feb when I had the crown put on. I wear my night guard every night. Xrays showed nothing wrong today. I am wondering if my night guard isn't fitting properly because it was fit for my mouth before the crown. I am going to wear a different night guard for my upper teeth and see if that helps. Can you always see the need for a root canal on an xray or could it be too early?

### Joann — 2012-06-22T08:55:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-do-about-a-root-canal.1025/post-32904>

oh, I see. Thanks for reply.

### Fara — 2012-06-22T16:06:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-do-about-a-root-canal.1025/post-32928>

> **vwaggs;33020 wrote:** Fara I have the crown because of the crack. It was actually in Feb when I had the crown put on. I wear my night guard every night. Xrays showed nothing wrong today. I am wondering if my night guard isn't fitting properly because it was fit for my mouth before the crown. I am going to wear a different night guard for my upper teeth and see if that helps. Can you always see the need for a root canal on an xray or could it be too early?

Vicki,

It depends on your symptoms. And it also could take some time for things to show up on X-rays.

Having a proper fitting NG is key to see if your symptoms go away.

If I may suggest, look for a Neuromuscular dentist in your area. One. Who is trained in correcting your bite so you don't clench or grind anymore. NG help, but you still continue grinding with a NG. With an orthotic made based on principals of optimized jaw position, you can eliminate your grinding/clenching.

You also mentioned you are a clincher. Usually clinchers have cervical issues that contribute to their clenching. This is something that a neuromuscular dentist can help with.

### vwaggs — 2012-06-22T17:12:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-do-about-a-root-canal.1025/post-32944>

That is very interesting! What kind of cervical issues? I assumed it was all stress related. I used to go to the chiropractor 3 times a week because I have no curve in my neck. Lifting weights has helped my pain and strengthened all the muscles in the area.

### Fara — 2012-06-22T18:35:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-do-about-a-root-canal.1025/post-32955>

> **vwaggs;33074 wrote:** That is very interesting! What kind of cervical issues? I assumed it was all stress related. I used to go to the chiropractor 3 times a week because I have no curve in my neck. Lifting weights has helped my pain and strengthened all the muscles in the area.

It is not all stress related. It has to do with how the first two vertebras (C1 andC2) are supporting the skull or lack there of. An A/O (Atlas orthogonal) chiropractor can help.

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-03T17:10:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-do-about-a-root-canal.1025/post-35279>

I had all of my amalgam fillings removed a couple of months ago because they were pulling away from the teeth. I had them for probably 20-25 years. Ever since my first one was replaced I have had sensitivity to cold in it. I was trying to give the tooth time to settle down by not chewing on that side and not drinking anything cold, but this weekend I had a really cold glass of water (not realizing how cold it was before drinking it) and now I am miserable. My whole mouth aches. So I went back to the dentist and it isn't the tooth I thought it was. It is the one behind it that is causing the pain. So now I've been given the option of a root canal or extraction. The tooth never bothered me until I had the tooth in front of it re-filled. I'm really confused as to what I should do. On one hand I just want the pain to go away but on the other, I really don't want to lose my tooth.

Any thoughts?

### castillonewsom — 2012-07-09T10:46:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-do-about-a-root-canal.1025/post-36299>

Dental implant is the best alternate option for root canal and you can go with this treatment. With this treatment, you will get artificial teeth instead of broken teeth and you will get permanent solution.

### Joann — 2012-07-09T22:35:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-do-about-a-root-canal.1025/post-36385>

> **kjgraffin;35439 wrote:** I had all of my amalgam fillings removed a couple of months ago because they were pulling away from the teeth. I had them for probably 20-25 years. Ever since my first one was replaced I have had sensitivity to cold in it. I was trying to give the tooth time to settle down by not chewing on that side and not drinking anything cold, but this
>
> weekend I had a really cold glass of water (not realizing how cold it was before drinking it) and now I am miserable. My whole mouth aches. So I went back to the dentist and it isn't the tooth I thought it was. It is the one behind it that is causing the pain. So now I've been given the option of a root canal or extraction. The tooth never bothered me until I had the tooth in front of it re-filled. I'm really confused as to what I should do. On one hand I just want the pain to go away but on the other, I really don't want to lose my tooth.
>
>
>
> Any thoughts?

Its so difficult making decisions about teeth since it is usually a permanent result!

seems pains travels all over in the mouth. I just had a root canal redone. I don't know if it was the shots or the draining of swelling on my palate but I had excruciating pain for 30 minutes all over one side up and down. while examining the root canal tooth x ray showed the tooth next to it has a filing real close to nerve. he did a sensitivity test by touching it with something very cold ....it stung. now the specialist thinks I should have a root canal in that tooth too.

I would have said ..when replacing fillings the hole gets bigger and therefor could be closer to the nerve.. but its tooth next to it which makes me suspicious of what is going on when it comes to teeth. maybe try some anti sensitivity tooth paste on tooth. Get a second opinion. I don't know if an implant is better than root canal. implant is titanium inside gum, root canal with crown has metal inside usually or I have one without metal. make sure root canal dentist is a specialist i think. (there was decay on tooth under crown in mine)

hope this helps making your decision, Good Luck!

### LisaLearning — 2015-07-29T11:59:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-do-about-a-root-canal.1025/post-173393>

Bumping this old thread since I was searching about some dental issues.

Jan, I am wowed that your teeth are more heavily restored than mine! I don't have any implants, but quite a few root treated teeth, and a lot of crowns. I have spent a lot of time trying to look after my teeth well, and this is the awful result, which I am sure is partly due to formerly terrible diet, but my husband (who also ate the same rubbish and who does the bare minimum with his teeth, just brushing 2x a day) has only two tiny composite fillings, sigh.

Now one of my premolar crowns has been leaking at the margin and I have enough decay (sadly below the bone line) that it looks like I will need to have it extracted and replaced with either a bridge or an implant.

Regarding the bridge choice, I am concerned that not putting any pressure on the bone in that area will lead to bone loss and possibly affect adjacent teeth, both of which are miraculously not root treated (but the adjacent molar is already crowned).

Regarding the possibility of an implant, I'm concerned about metal and just drilling something into the bone (maybe this is just irrational however, because people end up with metal pins of all sort in all sorts of places in the body, without ill effect, I think).

I'm also considering getting some of my crowns replaced in Poland. A friend's partner is Polish, and they always have their dentistry done when they are visiting her family, because (they tell me) the dental education is excellent in Poland now, and very up-to-date and better than here in England, and much much cheaper.

I'm originally from the US, and very much came to England worried about the legendary Big Book of British Smiles, and still don't know how I feel about UK dentistry. I paid £1300 (approx $2000!) from a fancy Harley St dentist (who did some graduate training at a dental school in Chicago, which I hoped meant he was up to date and knew what he was doing) several years ago for a porcelain on metal crown where the porcelain doesn't even cover the lingual side of the crown and so I am really not sure doing things here is the best choice anyway. I don't know how to find someone who really knows what they are doing to do the work, and I hoped that finding someone who had some US education (and charged a lot!) could be a decent proxy for skilled work, but that did not seem to work.

Lastly, if I do an implant, is titanium the best choice? I keep hearing about zirconium and I wonder if there is a non-metal remotely as strong (or not as brittle) as non-metal.

### Janette Layne — 2019-05-08T17:28:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-do-about-a-root-canal.1025/post-269938>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=30765)
>
> Teeth are my specialty.......I dont talk about them much here but i do on some consults and people are amazed about what they tell us. You want to save a tooth if you can but you need to dictate what the tooth is filled with and how its done.......once you know the details you can shop the endodontist/dentist

I am due for implants & had a root canal about six months ago that is still painful but nothing is showing up on the X-Ray. I would like a consultation. How do I arrange that?

### hazyjane — 2012-04-27T04:35:23+0300

>
>
>
> I am trying to lower the night one with PS and magnesium. I think it is getting better but am still waking up during the night and can feel the rush.

I would take cortisol modulators. Ashwaganda during the day is supposed to help modulate cortisol at night. It's also generally supportive of the adrenals.

### Destiny — 2012-04-27T22:07:02+0300

Thanks, hazyjane, I'll give it a try.

### 2bls — 2012-07-13T21:21:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recommendation-for-adrenal-glandular.1026/post-37566>

I've been trying Relora(300 mg) and Holy Basil(500 mg) along with 50-100 mg of B6 before bed and that seems to dial down the cortisol. I am not waking in the night(1-3 am) but the lightening of the sky wakes me between 5 and 5:30 am. I'm able to roll over and sleep for another hour or so...if I feel jittery from the cortisol spike in the am, I'll take another capsule of the Relora and the Holy Basil. I've had some pretty good nights of sleep(interesting dreams too!) since I started doing this. I don't even have to get up for the bathroom on these nights!

### Destiny — 2012-07-14T01:27:20+0300

I've had some pretty good nights of sleep(interesting dreams too!) since I started doing this. I don't even have to get up for the bathroom on these nights!

That is interesting. I would be happy to wake at 5 am instead of 2am. I've been taking Ashwaganda. Is it OK to add Relora and Holy Basil?

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/posting-here-as-this-seems-to-be-a-womens-area-interesting-maca-side-effect.1028/post-17400>

I got my MACA powder and added a bit to my BP coffee this a.m.

I swear I am having a hot flash from it!! I'm sitting here sweating! So, normally I feel too cold in the a.m. to think about CT - but I think I'm going to go plop down in the tub (full of the cold water from last nights bath, I use it about 4 times before changing).

Am I imagining this? Anybody else see this? It's also my first week to use MCT oil in my coffee - so It could be related to that too I suppose.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-21T16:52:05+0300

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-22T16:29:49+0300

I had to stop taking Maca because it was giving me sugar crashes?? Which is weird because Maca is listed under Adrenal Rx. I am assuming my sugar crashes have to do with adrenal glands, so why would something good for adrenals cash low blood sugar?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-22T16:33:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/posting-here-as-this-seems-to-be-a-womens-area-interesting-maca-side-effect.1028/post-17722>

Maca also affects cortisol......eventually. But you have to fix the underlying problem......taking Maca alone and not doing other things to fix the real underlying issue is like putting spackle in a car dent and say its fixed. Lots of modern humans think just that way......you cant out supplement or out exercise or out diet a poor cellular physiology.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-22T16:34:47+0300

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-22T20:37:39+0300

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-22T20:57:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/posting-here-as-this-seems-to-be-a-womens-area-interesting-maca-side-effect.1028/post-17826>

You need a real solid hack with testing.....you need to sit down and write out your history and give it to a doc and then talk with them.....come up with a game plan and test those areas and work from there. If you do not .......you will waste lots of time and money and raise your cortisol higher.......and you do not need that at all.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-23T17:59:22+0300

### Mud Flinger — 2012-04-24T17:41:10+0300

# Thread: How do you get used to it?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-get-used-to-it.1032/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 50
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Sem — 2012-04-21T17:17:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-get-used-to-it.1032/post-17419>

I've been trying to get used to CT but absolutely hate it. Hate hate hate it. Anyone who really had a hard time at first but made it through to the other side? Any suggestions? Thanks!

### Adria — 2012-04-21T18:14:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-get-used-to-it.1032/post-17434>

I'm going really slow to get used to it. Follow your own path. Since wk3 I started having positive results.. now I crave it. Keep pushing forward at your own speed and don't compare yourself to anyone else.

### Destiny — 2012-04-21T18:16:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-get-used-to-it.1032/post-17435>

> **Sem;17490 wrote:** I've been trying to get used to CT but absolutely hate it. Hate hate hate it. Anyone who really had a hard time at first but made it through to the other side? Any suggestions? Thanks!

You need to start at the point where you are somewhat comfortable and go from there. As recommended, I started with face dunks and could tolerate a lot. When I got to the bath taking, I had to start all the way up at 78 degrees. Gradually, I was going down. Now, I am comfortable at 58 degrees. In addition, I did not jump right in, I was sitting in the bathtub as water was pouring in. That is easier to do in my opinion. Also for the bath, have a hat and scarf. It helped me a lot.

It is not a linear process. Just take your time, you will get there.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-21T18:32:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-get-used-to-it.1032/post-17443>

> **Sem;17490 wrote:** I've been trying to get used to CT but absolutely hate it. Hate hate hate it. Anyone who really had a hard time at first but made it through to the other side? Any suggestions? Thanks!

I had done face dunks, max 2 seconds first time, got better. Next was ice packs on front & back while lying on a bed. Tried a shower--awful. I tried the frog method of cold tubbing-also bad. I hated getting straight in too.

Several weeks ago someone suggested getting the feet accustomed first. That was a huge help and I've been in the tub ever since. My tap water was 53 degrees at the time. I filled the tub, stood in it until the shock was over and my feet and ankles were numb, then sat down. I still had to be conscious of my breathing, but it was much easier than just going all in. Now I actually like it. My temp jumps when I get in and maxes at 2.5 above pre-bath temp.

I'd try the feet only first--even if you have to do it several times before sitting.

### Pamela — 2012-04-21T18:48:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-get-used-to-it.1032/post-17449>

I would say to make sure of a few things:

\-- Take the Bitter Melon as close to 2 hours before the bath as possible

\-- Make sure to eat a high fat meal prior to the bath

\-- For me personally being well rested was important, like not trying to do an ice bath on 5 hours sleep.

\-- For me personally doing it every other day is the right thing for now. Maybe I will get to every day but Dr. K says 3-4 times a week so that is how I am rolling now.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-04-21T18:58:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-get-used-to-it.1032/post-17455>

it just takes time to get used to it.

however, if you want to keep progressing it will never be pleasant.

Everytime I get to a certain time in the immersion bath and it isn't a big deal, I know it is time for me to either increase the time next time, or adjust the temp lower.

But the key to make it easier is just take it slow, the body will adjust.

### AKMan — 2012-04-21T19:33:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-get-used-to-it.1032/post-17481>

I was having a hard time with the baths. I could take -20 air, walking barefoot in the snow, and cold showers, but the baths did me in. I wanted to conquer it, so I did this week. I got down to 34 degrees for 45 minutes! Funny thing is, I was actually getting a rush doing it. Going colder and colder was like an obsession. Just imagine the feeling you get right before bungee jumping or skydiving. I could tell the adrenalin was pumping as the tub was filling.

Anyway, I posted this on the Beginners Guide yesterday--maybe it will help:

Here's a recommendation for newbies to CT. For your very first CT session, find a container big enough to stand in. Fill it with ice-cold water. Preferably knee-deep. Get that water as cold as you can, 32-33 degrees. Put in ice and cold water, the ice shouldn't melt, that's how cold you want it. You can even get it colder with salt! 31 degrees would be perfect.

Now, stand in the water. Note the feeling. It will feel like pin-pricks. The skin will turn brite red in about 30 seconds. Wiggle your toes. In about 2 minutes, they will be hard to wiggle as you lose motor control. Note how your ankles hurt, that's because they aren't protected by much muscle or fat. The pain will go away in a few minutes and numbness will take over.

Your first reaction will be to GET OUT. You may suck in your breath involuntarily, your heart may race. You might scream a little at first. Try to stay in at least 5 minutes. During this time, focus on your breathing. Pay attention to what this coldness feels like.

When you get out, let your feet drip-dry. Pay attention to how long it takes the redness to go away. You may notice goosebumps on your legs. A toe or two may turn white and go completely numb several minutes after you get out. This is OK, it will go away. If several toes go very numb, and you want feeling back quickly, soak your feet in some warm water for a few minutes. This is cause by your blood vessels constricting. You may also notice you start to shiver several minutes after getting out--usually not during. This is caused by the blood vessels opening back up and dumping that cold blood in your core, your body senses it and puts muscles to work to warm you up.

OK, so what did we just accomplish? You have just braved water that is as cold as any human (or any land mammal) has ever felt. Ever. Water has never been colder than this. And you survived. Do this a couple more times and you will find you can jump right in under a perfectly controlled situation. No yelping, gasping, wanting to jump out. Now that you know how the coldest water you will ever encounter feels, you are ready to proceed.

For your first big plunge, try 60 degree water--it will be like child's play compared to 32 degrees! I guarantee you will soon be down to 50 degrees and lower, and you will never get that nagging thought out of your head, 'what would a whole-body plunge at 32 degrees be like?'

I'm here to tell you, I did it this week--after CT'ing for about 2 months...and...It was awesome! The first night I stayed for 15 minutes, then warmed the water up to 55 degrees for another 30 minutes. The next night, I went 33 degrees for 45 minutes. Your legs will be stiff and you will shiver when you get out, but at least you'll know you can do it!

NOTE: Please ice responsibly, have a spotter and learn the signs of hypothermia--it's not worth die-ing over!

### Tucker — 2012-04-24T23:05:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-get-used-to-it.1032/post-18450>

> **Cavemam;18028 wrote:** I DID IT! Skin got to 33-34 and water down to 32. Ended up staying 30 mins easy. The most interesting thing to me was, aside from the gumption required to get in, ONCE I was in it was actually a easier than the 50 degree water.
>
>
>
> I made sure to do all the tricks: bitter melon, post meal, cold water, compression shirt.
>
>
>
> I did keep trying to stick my whole leg in, but the lower leg was really uncomfortable so I decided for 1st time not to push it. I was thinking I had not adapted much, but surely being able to sit down in 32 degree icy water for 30 mins means some kind of adaptation has taken place.
>
>
>
> I also think I'm experiencing some major post CT lady-hormone dump, but that's for another post.

Cavemam,

I totally agree with you that the colder the water is the easier it is to sit in the tub. I have been doing 30 min, full immersion CT at 54 degrees for over a month now. After listening to the webinar I decided it was time to do some deep CT. Today I did 25 minutes, total immersion (up to neck) at 40 degrees. I can't believe I went that long. Before I got in I was just hoping to be able to pull off 10 minutes. I sat in the 40 degree water and kept on thinking WOW this feels invigorating and I could go forever. Where as when I"m CTing in 54 degree water I"m constantly looking at my watch and thinking I can't wait to get out. I don't know if other people have experience a much more enjoyable experience at the colder temps but for me today it was like night and day. I'm obviously going to keep doing my CT at 40 degrees and will post back and let you know if I continue to feel this way about the colder water.

Today was the first time I experienced the red, cherry skin after getting out of the water. It almost looked like I had a deep tan. It took me about 2 hours to warm up and I did shiver for awhile afterward. It felt like I was doing involuntary, full body isometrics for the next couple hours, do you think that is why people get better body composition from CT?

Anyhow, I totally suggest everyone consider doing the deep CT. I"m completely surprised, in a good way with how it went. I don't know why it is just easier.

I have Hashimotos and adrenal fatigue. I always feel like a million bucks after my CT. For some reason it really helps me with my stomach. My stomach always feels more comfortable after CTing, like it helps with my digestion and I don't feel any bloating. Also, my brain fog is completely gone due to the CT.

I have some issues with rT3 and will be getting tested in a few more months to see if my numbers change. However, no testing is needed for me at this point to convince me that something good is happening. Deep down inside I feel this protocol is going to heal my autoimmunity.

So people get your deep CT on!!!!!!!!!!!!!!

### Tucker — 2012-04-25T00:02:52+0300
