The trouble I had was that I was making GAPS compliant yogurt, and keeping it at the right temp for 24 hours was a total PITA. Yogurt can turn out really strange if the temperature varies too much during the process.
>
>
>
> My MIL used to heat a large pot of milk, and when cooled to just the right temp she would add a cup of yogurt, and wrap warm towels and a blanket around the pot. Left alone overnight, she had perfect yogurt, but I don't know how much lactose would have been left in it.
>
>
>
> I really appreciated the yogurt maker from the first time I used it. So easy, and no messing around with hot water!

I basically do this except I put my yogurt in my crock from my crock pot and put in my oven with the temp set at 110. Some people's ovens won't go this low, I think mine will because it's a gas oven. I am planning on sitting down next week and doing a whole step by step guide to the yogurt making process and putting on my blog next week because I have some friends nd family who have been requesting it, but it really is quite easy once you've got the hang of it!

### David — 2012-04-27T23:39:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-made-yogurt-for-probiotics.1053/post-19447>

> **Trisha;19414 wrote:** ... I am planning on sitting down next week and doing a whole step by step guide to the yogurt making process ...

I will be looking forward to learning to do this. What is needed to get started?

### freesia — 2012-04-28T00:28:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-made-yogurt-for-probiotics.1053/post-19459>

I tuck mine up in a hot water cupboard (it has a hot water pipe running through it) with a hot water bottle. My mother always used a slowcooker or electric frypan but my grandmother just used a hot water bottle and a knitted woollen cosy. They made overnight yogurt but mine is cultured 24 hours. I've been making it regularly since 2007

I heat 1 liter of milk to barely a simmer, let cool to body temperature then stir in two Bio-kult capsules, or you can use 1/4 cup live yogurt as a starter, and pour into a sterilized jar, cover and pop to "bed". I have a stryofoam box positioned around the HW pipe with a hot water botter and thermometer in with the yogurt. Keep between 100 and 110F for 24 hours, cool, refrigerate...then it's ready.

I'm not having dairy at the moment but this is how I make it for my kids.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-28T06:15:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-made-yogurt-for-probiotics.1053/post-19524>

> **David;19520 wrote:** I will be looking forward to learning to do this. What is needed to get started?

I'll let ya know when I get it up. I want to take pics next time a make yogurt to go with the post.

### [email redacted] — 2012-05-24T19:24:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-made-yogurt-for-probiotics.1053/post-27056>

Sorry it took so long, but I finally got my recipe all put together and the post up!

[http://intoxicatedonlife.com/2012/05/24/make-your-own-healthy-yogurt-and-save-money/#](http://http://intoxicatedonlife.com/2012/05/24/make-your-own-healthy-yogurt-and-save-money/#)

# Thread: Chilblains
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chilblains.1054/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-04-22T18:40:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chilblains.1054/post-17774>

I have been doing CT for about 2 months, mostly face dunks, cold showers, and spot-icing, occasional 50-degree baths. About 2 weeks ago I developed red inflamed swollen itchy patches on my toes. After doing some research, it looks like what I have is Chilblains. Here is a description:

some people get chilblains when their skin gets cold. The tiny blood vessels under the skin narrow (constrict) when the skin becomes cold. The blood supply to areas of skin may then become very slow. As the skin re-warms there is some leakage of fluid from the blood vessels into the tissues. In some way this causes areas of inflammation and swelling leading to chilblains

I'm guessing that my exposure to cold caused the chilblains .

I'm at a loss as what to do next... Should I proceed with CT, or should I drop it? Is there a larger problem that needs to be addressed before I can proceed with this? It seems that this might somehow be connected to poor blood circulation, and week capillaries.. I also noticed that my hands are numb and have pins and needles every time I wake up during the night, and it takes a while to wake them up... Does the exposure to cold negatively affect blood circulation under certain circumstances?

I started Armour about a month ago, but I don't think that it's connected..I don't have diabetes, but my morning BG is around 105-110 every morning (falls to mid-80s at other times)

I'm wondering if anyone else has to deal with this?

I would appreciate any ideas/advice/suggestions as far as where to look for the underlying reason for this, and an advice on weather to proceed with CT or not.

Thank you. Elena

### AKMan — 2012-04-22T20:15:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chilblains.1054/post-17811>

When I lived in England, I heard lots of people complain about chilblains. It was thought to be caused by their humid, cold winters and cold, humid, drafty houses.

Not sure where you are in terms of diet, metabolism, supplements, vitamin status, and all that, but if you are 'broken', maybe you should take a little time to get your diet in order, get your Vit D checked and above 70, get on krill oil, clean up your diet. If you already have done that--sorry.

Otherwise, if you like CT, keep doing it, but be sure to dry and warm your feet and hands right away or just use spot-ice.

From Wikipedia:

Chilblains ( /ˈtʃɪlbleɪnz/; also known as pernio and perniosis)\[1\] is a medical condition that is often confused with frostbite and trench foot. Chilblains are acral ulcers (that is, ulcers affecting the extremities) that occur when a predisposed individual is exposed to cold and humidity. The cold exposure damages capillary beds in the skin, which in turn can cause redness, itching, blisters, and inflammation.\[2\] Chilblains are often idiopathic in origin but can be manifestations of serious medical conditions that need to be investigated. Chilblains can be prevented by keeping the feet and hands warm in cold weather. A history of chilblains is suggestive of a connective tissue disease.

Prevention

Avoid rapid changes in temperature.

Wear gloves and socks.

Use warm footwear.

Keep hands and feet warm.

Avoid tight fitting socks/shoes

Place cotton wool between the toes to improve circulation

Exercise to improve circulation

Avoid alcohol before going out in snow.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-22T20:58:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chilblains.1054/post-17827>

Akman, thank you for your response.

I'm broken health-wise, and working on fixing myself. I"m a 43-yr old female, who has been about 70 lbs overweight for the most part of my life, and couldn't get the weight off with any diet other than HCG (did 2 rounds of it and lost 40 lbs, that I've been struggling to keep off since October by doing Leptin Rx, paleo diet, and now CT). I know that all my hormones are low, and I was pre-diabetic prior to HCG despite being low-carb for the last 13 years, and I'm also hypothyroid, and was put on Armour about a month ago. My vit. D was 30 in Feb, I've been supplementing since, and will re-test in about a month. My cortisol is also too high in the morning and at night, and border-line low at noon and 5 pm. I'm taking adaptogens and PS for the adrenals, as well as b-vitamins. I'm a mess all-around, and, now, getting the chilblains, and weird numbness in my hands at night, I'm wondering if there is anything in particular in all this hormonal mess (or anything else that is non-hormonal), that might be provoking chilblains, and that I can address with some other supplement, or, if there is an underlying cause that might make somebody more prone to get them for any particular reason. I researched what I could about chilblains on the Internet, but couldn't find any explanation other then sharp change in cold/warm environment. But, from what I see, a lot of people on this forum is doing CT, but no-one is complaining about getting chilblains, so there must be some underlying reason for me getting them. If I learned anything from reading Dr.K's blog, the MDA thread, and this forum(all of which I'm extremely grateful for....), is that there is not a single symptom that doesn't have an underlying cause health-wise... So, I was wondering, if anyone else happened to have the same reaction to cold, or, if anyone can think of an explanation for it happening...

As far as CT, I'm enjoying it, although I don't feel any benefits yet, other than not being as cold as I used to be all the time because of low thyroid. I've been sleeping bad lately (waking up with heart pounding in my throat every hour or so), and not able to go back to sleep, in part, because of the itchy toes that are caused by chilblains. I thought that it might be related to thyroid/cortisol issues. Does anyone know if incorrect thyroid/adrenal treatment can cause poor circulation, which, in turn, can cause chilblains and numb arms/hands?

Thank you very much. Elena

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-22T23:25:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chilblains.1054/post-17886>

Hi tsatsa, I had those also, about 2 weeks into CT, they lasted about as long, I slowed down on the baths, and started to alternate 1 bath with feet/legs in, sitting upright, and reclining, with feet out of the tub.

I also upped my Krill from 500 to 1500 for those 2 weeks. I also took 2 warm baths with epsom salts, that helped with the dry skin, pimples and rough patches that started developing on my legs. My toes are fine now, and dryness is only a tiny patch on right ankle and left shin. HTH

# Thread: Kid's choice for bfast,  after farmer market day.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-choice-for-bfast-after-farmer-market-day.1055/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-22T18:51:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-choice-for-bfast-after-farmer-market-day.1055/post-17778>

We have been trying to rid ourselves of the "breakfast food" for breakfast idea with our child. Frankly, eggs everyday DO get boring.

His choice for bfast today - Salad with strawberries and Parmesan with balsamic dressing. Bowl of bone broth. Cup of raw milk.

MOM

### Sem — 2012-04-22T23:14:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-choice-for-bfast-after-farmer-market-day.1055/post-17884>

That's awesome! Congrats. What recipe do you use for bone broth?

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-23T04:06:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-choice-for-bfast-after-farmer-market-day.1055/post-17957>

I make both chicken and beef. I make a whole chicken once a week, and get enough brother to last a couple weeks and to have on hand. I 1 big chopped onion in the crockpot with a whole chicken and salt. Cook until meat is done. Remove meat, put all parts back in and cook on low for a minimum of 24 hrs. Strain and chill if you want the fat to come out. Then freeze!

### Sem — 2012-04-23T04:29:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-choice-for-bfast-after-farmer-market-day.1055/post-17961>

It is very hard though as it is a constant fight with everyone! The kids, the grandparents, the parties, the events. Would love any good tips!

all grains - no. It's hard because that is ALL the rest of the world eats!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-25T20:58:44+0300

### Sem — 2012-04-25T21:49:39+0300

### Sem — 2012-04-25T22:34:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-choice-for-bfast-after-farmer-market-day.1055/post-18831>

Actually, we did some testing at the allergy doctor and they reacted not only to nuts, but also to seeds (flax and sesame), dairy, wheat, corn, soy and my little one even reacted to RICE! Basically they are allergic to anything non-paleo. That's why I'm working so hard to get there. Except for forums like this, I don't have a lot of support. So thank you.

### BeingVenus — 2012-04-27T19:09:00+0300

# Thread: I am an animal!!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-am-an-animal.1057/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Mei — 2012-04-22T19:55:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-am-an-animal.1057/post-17806>

Hiya,

Where to begin...

Lately it's become clear that I have one main purpose in life, and a lot of smaller purposes that ultimately lead towards the same thing. I try to define it as a return to, or a reclamation of, our birthright - to be functional, dynamic, healthy, connected, beneficial, and joyful members of this earth. To fit in, as animals, to our planetary system, rather than being laid over top like the scab of destruction we seem to be at this time. Our species has always baffled me, and our blindness and commitment to suffering is a profound disease. I have always tried to calm my deep unease by exploring ways in which we might have a harmonic place in this world. I worked with animals, I studied Aikido and Zen, I took and ditched western philosophy, I wrote, I read, I prayed, I yearned. And sometimes I sank into deep, terrible sadness.

When I began working for a family trying to recover their child from Failure to Thrive (and possible autism indications) through the GAPS diet, I woke up to the power of food and the reality of nutritive health (the little boy recovered and continues to thrive, by the way, after two+ years of his mother's dedication). I studied all I could in between "real" (HA!) schoolwork, experimented on myself, got comfortable with the wonderful world of fats, found I could rescue myself from some anxiety and depression, lost a little weight, became a pretty good cook, got passionate about eating meat after being raised a life-long and animal-loving vegetarian...

...and I didn't quite realize I'd taken the red pill; nobody ever gave me a choice, there never was a blue pill. I was so excited to move forward that when I look back now, sometimes I am truly amazed at the paradigm shift I (and all of you, I'm sure) went through. But there has never been any other option, and every time I've tried to forget what I know and rejoin the masses, I've been rewarded with a massive stomach ache, a health plateau or crash, digestive issues for days or weeks...red flags from my body: "don't you f-ing DARE," it says. "I'm calling the shots now."

My goal in life is to live in a yurt with my man, dog, cats, and horses in the endless backyard of wild BC. Throw a goat or two in there, and it's gotta be by a large, cold body of water. I will hunt and fish and gather. I'm serious about this, there is no other future I can imagine, at least in which I am alive and well. Lucky, lucky me: my whole family and most of my friends would gladly follow. My further plan in life is to create a center for healing whatever ails you: come stay in my guest yurt, I will force enormous fat-breakfasts down your throat and kick you into 40 degree water, meanwhile you can hang out with my counselor mom and zen dad and activist brother and musician boyfriend and my wise, wise, horses and we'll see if we can't get you feeling a little better. I've always wanted to share my health and happiness, I've always known I am a healer in some regard, and I hope I can do that on a grander scale, one day. But having taken this darned red pill so early in life, I won't get a medical degree or a psychology degree or a nutrition degree, I won't have the patience to sit through years of bull\*\*\*\* in order to appear credible, I can't buy into it long enough so...I'm going to have to find another way.

Meanwhile, I eke out a frustrated living cooking GAPS/Paleo/WestonAPrice (hey, I'm grateful to be in good company) in the city, guzzling fossil fuels to visit my horses and get my nature fix, steadily healing my gut, brain, heart, and body, humorously hoping for a mysterious benefactor to buy me that acreage so I can start building my little home and get extra-serious about this circadian stuff...(PS...camping is perfect; wake up at six, eat cured meats and eggs, jump in the ocean, go for a walk, eat some more meats, go to sleep when it's dark out, feel like a forest queen!....if only that were daily life.)

Haha BUT wow, from GAPS to LeptinRx (I thought Jack was crazy, but tried it anyway to the best results I'd ever seen), to Primal Blueprint (during which I ended up addicted to fruit and honey...in the winter...not blaming anyone by myself, and it gave me the wake-up call I needed), at which point I returned to GAPS, then found CT, re-committed to the LeptinRx and now getting keto on top off it all...it's been quite the year.

I used to be:

\-much heavier than I felt; I knew who I was meant to be, knew the strong lithe creature I should have been, and was always shocked and dismayed by the reality of my outward image

\-vegetarian. HA!!!

\-depressed on a bipolar schedule

\-deeply anxious and emotional

\-disabled by my keen sensitivities

\-foggy, sleepy, anti-social, daunted by everything and anything, incapable of maintaining discipline or rhythms

\-afraid of water, unable to dunk my head despite a deep desire to be able to swim

I am now:

\-closer to my "real" self, leaner and stronger enough to believe in the process  Don't know weights because I'm too cheap to buy a scale, and it seems extremely arbitrary to me given that my main goal is, simply, optimal health (not a destination or a number that I will ever "reach")

\-energized on lots or little sleep

\-happy

\-inspired

\-resilient

\-calm as a cucumber

\-confident

\-leptin sensitive, I venture to declare

\-still a bad swimmer, but I'm in the ocean diving and splashing with the starfish in April while my dog and boyfriend shiver and whine on the rocks, can't complain...

Oh there is so much more to say. There is so much to be grateful for. There is so much to look forward to. I turn 22 today (on the 22nd, in 2012...!), and it feels very old and very young. But I am SO EXCITED, because I am on this stellar path. This ancient path, if I may...

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-22T20:18:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-am-an-animal.1057/post-17813>

Hi Mei and welcome to the road to optimal. I 'd love for you to post this to the Leptin Rx comments on the blog Mei if you would not mind......many more will see it there.

### Mei — 2012-04-22T20:39:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-am-an-animal.1057/post-17824>

Thanks Jack, and by the way, if it wasn't obvious, I don't think you're crazy anymore!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-22T20:59:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-am-an-animal.1057/post-17828>

Everyone is crazy at first because no one understands.......and then when they do the label changes. Labels are just labels. Your action determine your ultimate worth to people and not your words or your labels.

### Mei — 2012-04-22T21:09:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-am-an-animal.1057/post-17833>

Crazy definitely isn't a bad thing in my books, though...and if I ever said it about you it was with an incredulous grin and a lot of admiration...so maybe you're still crazy, and I've just gotten a little crazier myself. Crazy makes a lot more sense than safe, sick, and unsatisfied. It also goes well with other words: crazy-happy, crazy-good, crazy-awesome...

# Thread: what to say to the non-believers
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-say-to-the-non-believers.1058/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 25
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Mei — 2012-04-22T21:06:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-say-to-the-non-believers.1058/post-17830>

Hello you lovely people,

Have been asked how I could possibly have cured a cold with CT, when "everybody knows" you get a cold from...being cold! What's with idea that getting chilled brings on sickness? Is it true? If not, what's the deal?

I am constantly facing CW ideas that are, I think, unfounded, but I have no fact-power with which to refute them. Does anyone have any common sayings/belief (i.e. "Fat makes you fat, duh!") that they have have a calm, clever, concise, and irrefutable answer to? I realize I can't win against the great beast of common knowledge, but it would still be nice to stockpile on these. Maybe I'll make some flashcards

### Birdy — 2012-04-22T21:17:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-say-to-the-non-believers.1058/post-17840>

I really don't bother that much telling people about it. They were much more open to the leptin reset than CT, I think, because people are more used to doing crazy things to lose weight. The only thing that has worked is telling people that sports people use it. People are always trying crazy things elite athletes do. Everyone seems to want to overtrain and then make a fast recovery.

### David — 2012-04-23T00:08:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-say-to-the-non-believers.1058/post-17902>

I found this on line:

**The Cold, Hard Truth: Can Ice Baths Help You Lose Weight?**

March 1, 2011 10:51 PM

[http://minnesota.cbslocal.com/2011/03/01/can-ice-baths-help-you-lose-weight/](http://http://minnesota.cbslocal.com/2011/03/01/can-ice-baths-help-you-lose-weight/)

MINNEAPOLIS (WCCO) â€” Minnesotans love to complain about our winter weather, but we also know how to enjoy it. So, what if the secret to losing weight was actually buried in all that snow and ice?

Tim Ferriss says it is. He takes three ice baths a week, loading 20 pounds of ice into his tub to do something he calls thermal loading.

â€œIâ€™ll sit in an ice bath up to my waist for 10 minutes,â€ he said, â€œalso reading a book or magazine.â€

Ferriss wrote about it in his book, â€œThe 4-Hour Body,â€ and talked about it on the Doctor Oz show. He says the key is the cold.

â€œYour body wants to be 98.6 degrees, and if your body temperature is lowered, it will do everything it can to get back to 98.6 degrees. It burns calories as heat, and most of those calories come from fat,â€ he said.

Ferriss said you shouldnâ€™t start this plan cold turkey. Start with cold showers, work up to ice packs on your neck and then ice baths three times a week. But does his science hold water?

â€œWe are looking for these quick fixes,â€ says Dr. Stacy Ingraham, â€œno sweat equity to burn calories, and hereâ€™s yet another great example. You donâ€™t have to sweat because youâ€™re freezing to death.â€

She studies kinesiology and exercise physiology at the University of Minnesota. She said Ferriss has his science backwards, because cold actually slows down metabolism.

â€œTo burn the most calories,â€ according to Ingraham, â€œsauna and room temperature, not cold water immersion.â€

But what if you still bought into what Ferriss was saying? We decided to see what it was like and go ahead and take the plunge.

We filled Frank Vascellaroâ€™s tub and added the bags of ice. Frank said it didnâ€™t hurt initially, it just made him cringe. But a few minutes in, it starts to become painful in your toes and feet. And after eight minutes, Frank decided to call it quits.

Afterwards, it took him quite a while to shake the chill, which left us wondering about the dangers.

â€œLong-term exposure in cold water can start lowering your core body temperature and that can be very dangerous,â€ said Ingraham.

Thatâ€™s why ice baths for physical therapy are usually limited to 10 minutes.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-23T03:08:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-say-to-the-non-believers.1058/post-17943>

I've always thought this idea that the cold makes you sick is ridiculous. Since I was a teenager I haven't worn a jacket, not so much because I don't get cold but because I didn't want to have to keep track of it and bother with it. My mom is always fussing at me that I don't bundle my kids up enough and is afraid that they'll get sick. I keep trying to explain that the cold doesn't make you sick, but viruses do. Anyhow, I have a personal blog that I'm trying to use to explain to my friends and family some of these things I've been learning both on dr kruse blog as well as elsewhere. I'm trying to be sure to cite good reliable sources as well. My next post I am trying to finish up is on this subject of how the cold effects the immune system. There have been a couple of good studies. Dr kruses experiences are great anecdotal evidence as well. Just show your friends the tedx video!

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-24T04:42:09+0300

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-04-24T04:57:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-say-to-the-non-believers.1058/post-18263>

I find unlike with diet, CT I get incredulous praise. I get 'wow, you're crazy', but with a respect and "Wow, you're so brave and strong". No explanation needed for that. Food-wise it's always hell dealing with people who either are sick and unhealthy and eat bad food anyway but get offended if you don't eat their food...OR...I get "But you're slim, so why worry? (Well, I am NOW...)...OR...I get a person who might be 'naturally' lean and 'brag' that they can eat anything. Either way it's pretty frustrating...BUT I eat what I want to eat, what makes me feel good, what resets my hormones (and leptin). It would just be nice if people were cool with what I ate. That's all.

So +1 for CT!!

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-04-24T05:05:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-say-to-the-non-believers.1058/post-18268>

I should mention that the incredulous praise comes from when I get the chances to swim in Lake Ontario. People see me, and I wave vigorously and shout out: "Great day!" They say, "Isn't the water cold?!" I stop and look like I'm 'thinking' about it and say, "For me, no it's not: For you, yes it is."

### kathylu — 2012-05-05T18:09:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-say-to-the-non-believers.1058/post-21623>

Hey, I don't mean to be contrary here, but there is something to the "cold gives you colds" old wives tale. Colds are caused by viruses, but viruses attach to cell surfaces and infect more efficiently if body temperature drops below 97 degrees. So, IF you have been exposed to a virus and IF you get chilled and IF your immune system is subpar, you will catch cold more easily. I should know, I'm a biochemist specializing in virology.

### jsrhoades — 2012-05-06T15:58:16+0300

"Is that Core body temp?"

Key question. My core temp (per infrared ear thermometer) goes up (typically 99.5), not down when I'm doing a cold tub soak. It gradually comes down to 98.5 over 40-45 minutes. I haven't done cold soaks longer than that yet. Incidentally, I've had just one cold in the past three years after starting Paleo diet, and that was before I started dabbling with CT.

# Thread: Hangovers, glutathione,acetaldehyde
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hangovers-glutathione-acetaldehyde.1060/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-04-22T21:39:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hangovers-glutathione-acetaldehyde.1060/post-17851>

How Hangovers Work [http://science.howstuffworks.com/environmental/life/human-biology/hangover4.htm](http://http://science.howstuffworks.com/environmental/life/human-biology/hangover4.htm)

Biology of a Hangover: Acetaldehyde

A product of alcohol metabolism that is more toxic than alcohol itself, acetaldehyde is created when the alcohol in the liver is broken down by an enzyme called alcohol dehydrogenase. The acetaldehyde is then attacked by another enzyme, acetaldehyde dehydrogenase, and another substance called glutathione, which contains high quantities of cysteine (a substance that is attracted to acetaldehyde). **Together, the acetaldehyde dehydrogenase and the glutathione form the nontoxic acetate (a substance similar to vinegar). This process works well, leaving the acetaldehyde only a short amount of time to do its damage if only a few drinks are consumed.**

Unfortunately, the liver's stores of glutathione quickly run out when larger amounts of alcohol enter the system. This causes the acetaldehyde to build up in the body as the liver creates more glutathione, leaving the toxin in the body for long periods of time. In studies that blocked the enzyme that breaks down acetaldehyde (acetaldehyde dehydrogenase) with a drug called Antabuse, designed to fight alcoholism, acetaldehyde toxicity resulted in headaches and vomiting so bad that even alcoholics were wary of their next drink. Although body weight is a factor (see How Alcohol Works), part of the reason women should not keep up with men drink-for-drink is because women have less acetaldehyde dehydrogenase and glutathione, making their hangovers worse because it takes longer for the body to break down the alcohol.

Some of the most common hangover symptoms -- fatigue, stomach irritation and a general sense of illness all over -- can be further attributed to something called glutamine rebound. In the next section, we'll see what this aftereffect is all about.

### chocolate — 2012-04-23T15:44:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hangovers-glutathione-acetaldehyde.1060/post-18041>

[http://www.gastrojournal.org/article/S0016-5085%2800%2920235-7/abstract?referrer=http://scholar.google.com/scholar\_url?hl=en](http://http://www.gastrojournal.org/article/S0016-5085%2800%2920235-7/abstract?referrer=http://scholar.google.com/scholar_url?hl=en)

**Increased gastrointestinal ethanol production in obese mice: Implications for fatty liver disease pathogenesis

Background & Aims: Similarities in the hepatic responses to obesity and ethanol exposure suggest that these conditions evoke common pathogenic mechanisms. Thus, it is possible that ethanol exposure is increased in obesity. Given that intestinal bacteria can produce ethanol, the aim of this study was to determine if the intestinal production of ethanol is increased in obesity. Methods: Breath was collected from genetically obese, ob/ob male C57BL/6 mice and lean male littermates at different ages (14, 20, and 24 weeks) and times of the day (9 AM, 3 PM, and 9 PM). Obese mice (24 weeks old) were then treated with neomycin (1 mg/mL) for 5 days, and sampling was repeated. Results: Breath collected in the morning from 24-week-old obese mice had a higher ethanol content than breath from their lean littermates (271 vs. 78 pmol/mL CO2; P < 0.0001). Subsequent studies in 14- and 20-week-old mice showed that exhaled ethanol increased with age in obese (from 26 to 107 pmol/mL CO2; P < 0.002) but not lean (29 and 12 pmol/mL CO2) mice and was greater in older obese mice than in older lean mice (P < 0.0006). Obese mice showed a diurnal increase in breath ethanol in the morning that decreased through the afternoon and evening (107 to 33 to 13 pmol/mL CO2). Neomycin treatment decreased morning breath ethanol levels by 50%\[B/\] (from 220 to 110 pmol/mL CO2; P < 0.0003). **Conclusions: Even in the absence of ethanol ingestion, ethanol can be detected in exhaled breath. In obesity, an age-related increase in breath ethanol content reflects increased production of ethanol by the intestinal microflora. Hence, intestinal production of ethanol may contribute to the genesis of obesity-related fatty liver.**

Neomycin appeared to cut the production in half. It really drives home the gut flora argument.**

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Durian](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Durian)

In 1981, J. R. Croft wrote in his Bombacaceae: In Handbooks of the Flora of Papua New Guinea that "a feeling of morbidity" often follows the consumption of alcohol too soon after eating durian. Several medical investigations on the validity of this belief have been conducted with varying conclusions,\[4\] **though a study by the University of Tsukuba finds the fruit's high sulphur content caused the body to inhibit the activity of aldehyde dehydrogenase, causing a 70% reduction of the ability to clear toxins from the body.**

### chocolate — 2012-08-22T20:42:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hangovers-glutathione-acetaldehyde.1060/post-46393>

[http://drlwilson.com/Articles/FERMENTS.htm](http://http://drlwilson.com/Articles/FERMENTS.htm)

> PROBLEMS WITH FERMENTED FOODS
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> 1\. Too yin for several reasons. Ferments, which are yeasts, are extremely yin organisms. They are often anerobes that use a glucose-based metabolism. This makes the food more yin, generally.
>
>
>
> Also, the food is eaten in an essentially raw state. This also makes the food much more yin, in general, than if the food were cooked. It is true that some fermented foods are less sweet and more acidic, which adds some yang energy to them, but not too much in many cases. In general, fermented foods are much more yin, as a general rule, than the original food before it was fermented.
>
>
>
> To read more about this very important aspect of foods, read Yin And Yang Healing on this website.
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> 2\. Aldehyde poisoning. Aldehydes are a type of organic chemical compound that are produced by most fermenting organisms, not just wine and beer. Aldehydes are toxic to the body, and this is a major problem with all fermented foods, bar none. To eat these foods in anything but small quantities is not healthful at all.
>
>
>
> **On hair mineral tests, we see the results of eating aldehydes. The person’s sodium/potassium ratio starts to decline and the vitality diminishes. This is a consistent finding. The more aldehydes one eats, the worse the problem.**
>
>
>
> The worst foods for the production of aldehydes are wine, beer, and some cheeses. The least are produced in foods such as miso, which is fine as a soup base, especially because one does not eat a lot of it.

### chocolate — 2012-08-26T04:32:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hangovers-glutathione-acetaldehyde.1060/post-47182>

[http://www.drkaslow.com/html/leaky\_gut.html](http://http://www.drkaslow.com/html/leaky_gut.html)

> **In a healthy situation the small intestine epithelium maintains tight cell junctions, which contributes to the physical barrier involved in intestinal absorption. In other words the cells are kept close together so nothing can penetrate between them. Candida exudes an aldehyde secretion that causes the cells ling the small intestine (epithelial cells) to shrink.** This allows intestinal toxins to pass by the epithelium and enter the tissues and blood. In addition to the physical barrier, there is an important chemical barrier within the mucus that contains immune agents which neutralize any toxin that comes in contact. This mucus-immune barrier remains as the sole defender, which eventually becomes overwhelmed.
>
>
>
> Although many people believe Candida itself enters the blood stream allowing it to be deposited elsewhere, such as the brain, unless the immune response is completely depleted, as in AIDS, Candida is quickly destroyed in the blood. The real damage done by Candida is to the intestinal epithelial barrier, allowing the absorption of toxins agents and chemicals, which then enter the blood and affect numerous organs, including the brain.

Greeting Team!

I have a new forum assignment for my fellow CT travellers (including yourself Dr. K).

It has been shown via various research papers that Haplogroup I is more predisposed to conditions related to immune response compared to other haplogroup types - including conditions like CAD (Coronary Artery Diease). Haplogroup I tends to live in Northern Europe. Could ancient, pre-modern cold conditions have kept us alive? We seem to be less prevalent in other climates. Here is a link to a news story:

[http://www.thehindu.com/health/medicine-and-research/article2872513.ece](http://http://www.thehindu.com/health/medicine-and-research/article2872513.ece)

# Thread: What are apartment/city dwellers doing for more intense CT sessions?

### BeingVenus — 2012-04-22T22:12:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-apartment-city-dwellers-doing-for-more-intense-ct-sessions.1062/post-17866>

My husband, son and I live in an apartment in the downtown of a new England city. For the past week or two I've been doing CT by taking cold showers, sitting in the tub with bags of Ice in the waters And a few ice packs on my abdomen and neck area. Then the last 5-10 minutes I sit cross legged with my face submerged. My tub isn't really big enough for me to get more than half-2/3 of me cold and my husband is so tall he can't even sit in it. I'm really envious of those of you with a backyard to put in a tub big enough for full body submersion. Has anyone figured out an apartment hack for more intense CT? We dont have a balconey and I'd do the ocean dip since its perfect temperature right now but I am a stay at home mom and can't really bring the 1 year old in the ocean for a full hour.

### chocolate — 2012-04-22T23:57:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-altitude-and-the-endocrine-system.1064/post-17896>

[http://theoncologist.alphamedpress.org/content/8/suppl\_1/15.full](http://http://theoncologist.alphamedpress.org/content/8/suppl_1/15.full) from the oncologist....entire article is good....Type A blood does not have sialic acid

The Cellular Biology of Erythropoietin Receptors

Approximately 60% of the erythropoietin molecule is protein and the remainder is carbohydrate. Each of the four carbohydrate groups (Fig. 2⇑) consists of a small, branched chain of sugars, some of which terminate in sialic acid. The presence of sialic acid on the carbohydrate slows the rate of clearance of erythropoietin by the liver. Clearly, the longer erythropoietin remains in the circulation, the greater the opportunity for it to stimulate erythroid progenitor cells located in the bone marrow.

### chocolate — 2012-04-23T00:23:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-altitude-and-the-endocrine-system.1064/post-17907>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/18800958](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/18800958) High Altitude versus low altitude Sherpas

Impaired glucose regulation in a Sherpa indigenous population living in the Everest region of Nepal and in Kathmandu Valley.

Abstract

The aim of this study was to determine the prevalence of impaired glucose regulation status in Sherpa adults living in the Everest area and in Kathmandu valley. A cross-sectional survey was conducted in Chaurikharka village (Everest area) and Kathmandu city on 119 and 121 randomly selected individuals, aged 30-70 years. They were assessed on conventional risk factors for diabetes, and an oral glucose tolerance test was performed. Based on the 2003 American Diabetes Association criteria, the prevalence in the Kathmandu city and Everest region of any impaired glucose regulation (IGR), isolated impaired fasting plasma glucose (isolated IFG), isolated impaired glucose tolerance (isolated IGT), and combined isolated IFG and isolated IGT were 55.4% vs. 23.5%, 42.1% vs. 14.3%, 1.7% vs. 0.8%, 11.6 vs. 8.4%, respectively. **Using the subjects with normal glucose tolerance as the referent group and after adjusting for age, sex, physical activity, calories, and waist circumference, the odds ratios for isolated IFG and combined isolated IFG and isolated IGT of living in the highland region were 0.19 (0.08-0.44) and 0.33 (0.09-1.18), respectively. Isolated IFG was more common among the lowland Sherpas. Unlike combined isolated IFG and isolated IGT, this isolated IFG difference could not be explained by the difference of conventional diabetes mellitus risk factors.

I wish I could find the Sherpa's blood types. Second generation lowland sherpas adapt more quickly to the altitude than others.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/17971439](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/17971439)

Higher blood flow and circulating NO products offset high-altitude hypoxia among Tibetans.

Erzurum SC, Ghosh S, Janocha AJ, Xu W, Bauer S, Bryan NS, Tejero J, Hemann C, Hille R, Stuehr DJ, Feelisch M, Beall CM.

Source

Department of Pathobiology, Allergy, and Critical Care, Cleveland Clinic, Cleveland, OH 44195, USA.

Abstract

The low barometric pressure at high altitude causes lower arterial oxygen content among Tibetan highlanders, who maintain normal levels of oxygen use as indicated by basal and maximal oxygen consumption levels that are consistent with sea level predictions. This study tested the hypothesis that Tibetans resident at 4,200 m offset physiological hypoxia and achieve normal oxygen delivery by means of higher blood flow enabled by higher levels of bioactive forms of NO, the main endothelial factor regulating blood flow and vascular resistance. The natural experimental study design compared Tibetans at 4,200 m and U.S. residents at 206 m. Eighty-eight Tibetan and 50 U.S. resident volunteers (18-56 years of age, healthy, nonsmoking, nonhypertensive, not pregnant, with normal pulmonary function) participated. Forearm blood flow, an indicator of systemic blood flow, was measured noninvasively by using plethysmography at rest, after breathing supplemental oxygen, and after exercise. The Tibetans had more than double the forearm blood flow of low-altitude residents, resulting in greater than sea level oxygen delivery to tissues. In comparison to sea level controls, Tibetans had >10-fold-higher circulating concentrations of bioactive NO products, including plasma and red blood cell nitrate and nitroso proteins and plasma nitrite, but lower concentrations of iron nitrosyl complexes (HbFeIINO) in red blood cells. This suggests that NO production is increased and that metabolic pathways controlling formation of NO products are regulated differently among Tibetans. These findings shift attention from the traditional focus on pulmonary and hematological systems to vascular factors contributing to adaptation to high-altitude hypoxia.**

### chocolate — 2012-04-23T00:30:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-altitude-and-the-endocrine-system.1064/post-17909>

[http://jeb.biologists.org/content/215/2/256.abstract](http://http://jeb.biologists.org/content/215/2/256.abstract)

High-altitude diving in river otters: coping with combined hypoxic stresses

SUMMARY

River otters (Lontra canadensis) are highly active, semi-aquatic mammals indigenous to a range of elevations and represent an appropriate model for assessing the physiological responses to diving at altitude. In this study, we performed blood gas analyses and compared blood chemistry of river otters from a high-elevation (2357 m) population at Yellowstone Lake with a sea-level population along the Pacific coast. Comparisons of oxygen dissociation curves (ODC) revealed no significant difference in hemoglobin-oxygen (Hb-O2) binding affinity between the two populations - potentially because of demands for tissue oxygenation. Instead, high-elevation otters had greater Hb concentrations (18.7 g dl-1) than sea-level otters (15.6 g dl-1). **Yellowstone otters displayed higher levels of the vasodilator nitric oxide (NO), and half the concentration of the serum protein albumin, possibly to compensate for increased blood viscosity. Despite compensation in several hematological and serological parameters, theoretical aerobic dive limits (ADL) were similar between high-elevation and sea-level otters because of the lower availability of O2 at altitude.** Our results suggest that recent disruptions to the Yellowstone Lake food web could be detrimental to otters because at this high elevation, constraints on diving may limit their ability to switch to prey in a deep-water environment.

KEY WORDS

albumin

chloride shift

hemoglobin binding affinity

invasive species

Lontra canadensis

nitric oxide

oxygen dissociation curve

### chocolate — 2012-05-06T21:52:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-altitude-and-the-endocrine-system.1064/post-21808>

[http://journals.lww.com/nephrologytimes/blog/breakingnews/pages/post.aspx?PostID=40](http://http://journals.lww.com/nephrologytimes/blog/breakingnews/pages/post.aspx?PostID=40)

Altitude Adjustment in Cardiovascular Risk Observed for Dialysis Patients

Dialysis patients living at high altitudes have lower cardiovascular event rates, reported a recent study published online ahead of print by Nephrology Dialysis Transplantation.

Cardiovascular risk was 20% lower for patients residing at 4,000-5,999 feet and 19% lower for patients residing at 6,000 ft or higher, compared with otherwise similar patients who lived at or near sea level, found the researchers, who were led by Wolfgang C. Winkelmayer, MD, ScD, of Stanford University School of Medicine.

This work built on the researchers' previous observation that US dialysis patients and members of the general population who resided at higher altitudes had lower all-cause mortality rates.

In the new study, rates of myocardial infarction, stroke, and cardiovascular death were 31%, 32%, and 23% lower, respectively, among patients living above 6,000 ft compared with patients residing at or near sea level.

Dr. Winkelmayer and colleagues studied all adult patients from the US Renal Data System with sufficient data who initiated hemodialysis treatment between 1995 and 2006. Of the 984,265 patients included, almost 95% resided below 2,000 ft (40.3% below 250 ft, and 54.5% at 250-1,999 ft), and 4,356 patients (0.4%) resided above 6,000 ft.

Patients who lived at higher altitudes were younger, less likely to be covered by Medicaid, and more likely to be on peritoneal dialysis at the initiation of therapy.

While rates of most comorbid conditions were similar across altitude groups, patients at higher altitudes had more diagnosed diabetes and hypertension, slightly less arteriosclerotic heart disease and heart failure, and few instances of reported inability to ambulate or transfer.

There were 574,063 deaths observed, 257,955 (44.93%) of which were reported as cardiovascular. There was no meaningful association between altitude and non-cardiovascular death.

“The fact that altitude is shown to selectively attenuate the risk of cardiovascular mortality in dialysis patients suggests that there are underlying biological mechanisms that are activated at higher altitudes to protect these patients from cardiovascular risk,” the authors wrote. “These findings need to be considered with caution, however, and residual confounding remains a possibility.”

The inverse relationship between altitude and cardiovascular outcomes was examined in multivariable models.

“**This observation was only slightly attenuated even after adjusting for a large number of patient characteristics and is consistent with previous studies that have demonstrated the protective effect of HIF \[hypoxia-inducible factor\]-1 activation on the cardiovascular system,” the authors wrote. “We propose that HIF-1 plays an important role in conferring a protective effect on cardiovascular outcomes in dialysis patients.”**

### chocolate — 2012-05-15T17:03:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-altitude-and-the-endocrine-system.1064/post-24218>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21465908](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21465908)

Endocrine aspects of high altitude acclimatization and acute mountain sickness.

Abstract

The acute acclimatization to high altitude is underpinned by a diuresis (and to a lesser extent a natriuresis) that facilitates a reduction in plasma volume. This allows a haemoconcentration to occur that increases the oxygen carrying capacity of a given volume of blood, a vital effect in the presence of a reduced partial pressure of oxygen. This critical acclimatization process is orchestrated by the endocrine system. This review will present the key evidence regarding the changes in several important hormones that affect this process.

### chocolate — 2012-05-15T17:06:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-altitude-and-the-endocrine-system.1064/post-24220>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21394641](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21394641)

Effects of altitude exposure on brain natriuretic peptide in humans.

Woods D, Hooper T, Hodkinson P, Ball S, Wakeford R, Peaston B, Bairsto C, Green N, Mellor A.

Source

Newcastle and Northumbria NHS Trust, Newcastle upon Tyne, UK. [[email redacted]](mailto:[email redacted])

Abstract

**Acute mountain sickness (AMS) is common at high altitude (HA) and associated with a relative failure of the natriuresis and diuresis that occurs at HA. The role of Brain Natriuretic Peptide (BNP) in this context has not been thoroughly investigated**. We aimed to clarify if BNP rises in response to exercise at HA and if so whether this is related to AMS. 32 healthy subjects had assessments of BNP, aldosterone and AMS scores \[as assessed by the AMS-C score of the Environmental Symptom Questionnaire (ESQ) and Lake Louise questionnaire\] made following exertion at sea-level (SL), 3,400, 4,300 and 5,150 m. Data were analysed in the 23 subjects who did not consume drugs known to affect acclimatization. BNP (pg/ml, mean Â± SEM) was significantly higher at 5,150 m versus the lower altitudes (p < 0.001 for all): 7.1 Â± 1; 6.1 Â± 0.3; 6.8 Â± 0.9 and 17.7 Â± 5.1 at sea-level; 3,400, 4,300 and 5,150 m. In those that showed a BNP response at 5,150 m (n = 19) versus those that did not demonstrate a BNP response (n = 4) there was a significant difference in Lake Louise (LL) AMS scores at 5,150 m on day 10 of the expedition (mean LL score 3.3 vs. 0.75, p = 0.034) and day 11 (mean LL score 3.3 vs. 0, p = 0.003). This is the first report to demonstrate a significant rise in BNP at HA. A BNP response at 5,150 m may be associated with a greater likelihood of suffering AMS.

### chocolate — 2012-06-06T16:36:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-altitude-and-the-endocrine-system.1064/post-29705>

[http://jap.physiology.org/content/92/4/1573.abstract?cited-by=yes&legid=jap;92/4/1573](http://http://jap.physiology.org/content/92/4/1573.abstract?cited-by=yes&legid=jap;92/4/1573)

Changes in MCT 1, MCT 4, and LDH expression are tissue specific in rats after long-term hypobaric hypoxia

Little is known about the effect of chronic hypobaric hypoxia on the enzymes and transporters involved in lactate metabolism. We looked at the protein expression of monocarboxylate transporters MCT 1, MCT 2, and MCT 4, along with total lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) and LDH isozymes in skeletal muscle, cardiac muscle, and liver. Expression of these components of the lactate shuttle affects the ability to transport and oxidize lactate. We hypothesized that the expression of MCTs and LDH would increase after acclimation to high altitude (HA). The response to acclimation to HA was, however, tissue specific. In addition, the response was different in whole muscle (Mu) and mitochondria-enriched (Mi) fractions. Heart, **soleus, and plantaris muscles showed the greatest response to HA. Acclimation resulted in a 34% increase in MCT 4 in heart and a decrease in MCT 1 (−47%) and MCT 4 (−47%) in plantaris Mu. In Mi fractions, the heart had an increase (+40%) and soleus a decrease (−40%) in LDH. HA also had a significant effect on the LDH isozyme composition of both the Mu and Mi fractions. Mitochondrial density was decreased in both the soleus (−17%) and plantaris (−44%) as a result of chronic hypoxia. We conclude that chronic hypoxia had a tissue-specific effect on MCTs and LDH (that form the lactate shuttle) but did not produce a consistent increase in these components in all tissues.

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plantaris\_muscle](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plantaris_muscle)

It is composed of a thin muscle belly and a long thin tendon. It is approximately 2-4 inches long, and is absent in 7-10% of the human population.** It is one of the plantar flexors in the superior compartment of the leg along with the gastrocnemius, and soleus. The plantaris is considered an unimportant muscle and mainly acts with gastrocnemius.\[1\]

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Soleus\_muscle](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Soleus_muscle)

In humans and some other mammals, the soleus is a powerful muscle in the back part of the lower leg (the calf). It runs from just below the knee to the heel, and is involved in standing and walking.

### chocolate — 2012-06-24T20:44:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-altitude-and-the-endocrine-system.1064/post-33321>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21542052](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21542052)

Acetyl-L-carnitine-mediated neuroprotection during hypoxia is attributed to ERK1/2-Nrf2-regulated mitochondrial biosynthesis.

Hota KB, Hota SK, Chaurasia OP, Singh SB.

Source

High Altitude Physiology Division, Defence Institute of High Altitude Research, C/o 56 APO, Leh, Jammu and Kashmir, India.

Abstract

Neuronal damage in hypoxia and several neurodegenerative disorders is invariably associated with oxidative damage and mitochondrial dysfunction. Administration of acetyl-L-carnitine (ALCAR) on the other hand attenuates neuronal damage, prevents apoptosis, and improves energy status in hypoxic stress through less understood mechanisms. Becasue mitochondrial biogenesis could be a possible mechanism for ALCAR-induced improvement in bioenergetics in neurons, the present study aimed at exploring signaling pathways of ALCAR-induced neuroprotection in hypoxia and possible occurrence of mitochondrial biogenesis. To create global hypoxia, adult Sprague-Dawley rats were exposed to a simulated altitude of 7,620 m at standard temperature and humidity conditions. We here demonstrate that administration of ALCAR to hypoxic rats for a period of 2 weeks effectively protected hippocampal neurons from mitochondrial dysfunction, excitotoxicity, and neurodegeneration. ALCAR administration resulted in peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor γ coactivator-1α and nuclear respiratory factor-1-induced mitochondrial biogenesis, the expression of which was regulated by an extracellular-related kinase-nuclear factor erythroid 2-related factor 2 (ERK-Nrf2)-mediated mechanism. Most notably, calcium buffering into nonfunctional mitochondria ameliorated excitotoxicity and improved bioenergetic status of the hippocampal neurons. Together, the data reveal the immense therapeutic potential of ALCAR for the treatment of ischemia, stroke, and other neurodegenerative disorders associated with hypoxic stress and excitotoxicity.

### chocolate — 2012-06-24T20:50:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-altitude-and-the-endocrine-system.1064/post-33324>

[http://edulife.com.br/dados%5CArtigos%5CNutricao%5CNutricao%20Esportiva%20e%20Suplementacao%5CSupplementation%20of%20L-Carnitine%20in%20Athletes.pdf](http://http://edulife.com.br/dados%5CArtigos%5CNutricao%5CNutricao%20Esportiva%20e%20Suplementacao%5CSupplementation%20of%20L-Carnitine%20in%20Athletes.pdf)

> **Inhibition of Apoptosis in Immune Cells by L-Carnitine
>
> Signal transduction via the surface glycoprotein Fas, also known as
>
> CD95, is considered the most important pathway for the regulation
>
> of programmed cell death (i.e., apoptosis). L-carnitine inhibits
>
> apoptosis by interaction with the Fas ligand and the Fas-receptor
>
> systems.86 Signal transduction via the Fas receptor activates acid
>
> sphingomyelinase (in lysosomes), as a consequence, a breakdown
>
> of sphingomyelin and a release of ceramide occur. Immediate
>
> inhibition of acid sphingomyelinase has been shown under such
>
> conditions in vivo and in vitro.11 In addition, an inhibition of
>
> caspases 3, 7, and 8 and an inhibition of the so-called mitochondrial
>
> permeability transition could be induced by L-carnitine addition.
>
> 10,87 Another antiapoptotic mechanism of carnitine was detected
>
> in T lymphocytes, where addition of L-carnitine and, hence,
>
> a reduction of ceramide stimulated the level of insulin-like growth
>
> factor-1 in serum.88 Insulin-like growth factor inhibits dimerization
>
> of apoptosis regulating proteins BCL-2–BAX in the mitochondrial
>
> membrane.89 It also inhibits activation of transcription
>
> involving the BCL-2 promoters.** 90 An overview on the role of
>
> L-carnitine in regulation of apoptosis is shown in Figure 2.

### chocolate — 2012-06-24T21:09:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-altitude-and-the-endocrine-system.1064/post-33326>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/11133888](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/11133888)

> Alterations in enzymes involved in fat metabolism after acute and chronic altitude exposure.
>
> Kennedy SL, Stanley WC, Panchal AR, Mazzeo RS.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Department of Kinesiology and Applied Physiology, University of Colorado, Boulder, Colorado 80309, USA.
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> The purpose of this study was to examine the effect of acute (24 h) and chronic (5 wk) hypobaric hypoxic exposure equivalent to a simulated altitude of 4,300 m (446 mmHg) on the enzymes of fat metabolism. Heart, liver, and skeletal muscle were taken from 32 male Sprague-Dawley rats. Altitude exposure did not affect the activity of citrate synthase in any of the tissues, suggesting that mitochondrial content was unchanged. Carnitine palmitoyltransferase-I (CPT-I) activity was significantly reduced in the heart by both acute and chronic high altitude exposure compared with controls. A similar reduction was found for CPT-I activity in extensor digitorum longus after acute and chronic exposure compared with control animals. **CPT-I activity was not affected by altitude exposure in the soleus muscle or the liver. 3-Hydroxyacyl-CoA dehydrogenase (beta-HAD) activity was significantly depressed in the hearts of chronically exposed animals compared with controls. No difference between acute and control animals was found in the heart for beta-HAD activity. Liver beta-HAD activity was also significantly decreased in the acclimatized as well as in the acute animals compared with the control group. Quadriceps beta-HAD activity was reduced for the chronic animals only compared with controls. These data suggest that acclimatization to high altitude selectively decreases key enzymes in fat utilization and oxidation in the heart, liver, and select skeletal muscles.**
